author: @solandri69
text:
My structural engineering prof actually covered this in class (in 1989, so before that paper).  The analogy he used was to a ball rolling on a sinusoidal hill.  If the ball is placed at the very top of a peak or the exact bottom of a trough, the slope is zero so it is stable and stays there.  These correspond to spinning about the maximum and minimum moments of inertia.  But if you place the ball in between either of these points, it will try to roll towards the minimum, overshoot, head towards the maximum, not make it, come back down to the minimum, overshoot, repeat.


Same thing goes on with a rotating body.  It *wants* to rotate around the minimum or maximum moment of inertia, so it starts trying to change its rotational axis to match one of these axes.  But when the two match, the rate of change of its rotational axis s non-zero. So the rotation axis carries past that stable axis (overshoots), causing it to again end up rotating about a non-stable axis, and repeating the cycle.


While the third axis orthogonal to the maximum and minimum inertia axes is the most obvious, the same thing will happen if you rotate it around *any* axis which isn't the maximum or minimum.  (Analogous to starting the ball at any point on the hill other than the maximum, minimum, or the midpoint between those two.)

author: @tillotman
text: Great explanation, but one error at 8:20:  You claim that in the rotating frame, the outward centrifugal forces are balanced by the inward centripetal force, but in the rotating frame, the object is stationary, so there IS no centripetal force. (Symmetry: if there is no centrifugal force in the inertial frame, then there is no centripetal force in the rotating frame.) The inward force balancing the outward centripetal force is just the structural support (strain) of the disk itself.  When the smaller objects are perturbed off axis, then there is no analagous restoring force from the disk, so the motion is unstable.

author: @MarcusAndersonsBlog
text:
The fluid dynamics of the earth's liquid outer core and massive solid inner core have rotational instability due to the equatorial bulges of both the crust/mantle and the solid inner core. Like the mantle, the inner core is also an oblate ellipsoid and rotates within the liquid outer core slightly faster east than the  crust/mantle. This raises the prospect of an inversion of the inner core  independently of the  crust/mantle .  It also suggests that magnetic field reversal (in the magnetic outer core) could be a consequence of the physical inversion of the non-magnetic inner core .

The earth's crust/mantle has a 26000 year period of its 23.5° wobble.  If this wobble is spiraling (to or away from its wobble-pole) then it could well result in a slow flip of the earth over in a just few more wobble cycles (50,000 years).  Likewise, the magnetic (outer core) pole is drifting north west relative to the earth's surface, while the earth's inner core has a 60 year period of rotation east in a 10° wobble relative to the earth's mantle. However the magnetic north pole is (geologically) accelerating north west at high speed and if the  Dzhanibekov effect is thrown into the mix then a model could be devised showing the earth is indeed entering a period of high instability of its magnetic field due to interactions between the eccentric inverting inner core, the magnetic outer core and the rotation of the mantle.

This is not just a likely scenario. Almost every large object in our solar system revolves and rotates in an anti-clockwise direction. Uranus however has an almost horizontal (98°) inclination off the vertical making it either a rare clockwise rotation or inverted and just over half way through an inversion cycle. Venus is completely retrograde (clockwise) suggesting it has re-stabilised in the inverted state. Pluto sits at 120° or 60° retrograde. All the other planets have about 25° wobble except Jupiter at 3°, and only Mercury has no significant wobble (0°). 

This is visualised In the following link - as each planet tilts from the vertical to its current axis it unintentionally shows what is going on, albeit on a geological timescale --> https://youtu.be/GY6Pya_0OuU 

Planetary inversion is real.

text:
I can flip my phone without this happening like 25% of the time. doesn’t the force applied result in how the object flips? For example on the wing nut, when it is unscrewing the thread is bigger on one side than the other causing it to be unbalanced when it pops off carrying that angular momentum with it. In my mind the forces applied to unscrew will also play a roll as to how the energy is transferred causing the nut to be more energy oriented on one side than the other, I.e smacking a ruler on the ride that is hanging off the table. If all these forces were perfectly balanced and there are no instabilities will the tennis racket effect still happen. And if so why is it I can flip my phone which has the correct amount of points to pull off the effect perfectly every once in a while. I seem to be confused.


Update: i tested this by letting a cellphone go from a ladder onto a pillow below and used the slow motion feature on my phone to record and  was lucky enough to nail the perfect flip on my first try. eventually eventually the phone becomes unstable. I will write this off as 1. My hands are an inconsistent way of applying force and 2. The iPhone (though a nice rectangle) is an unbalanced object. Let’s say I solve problems one and two by having a 100% consistent way of releasing a perfectly balanced piece of steel rectangle. I then have to deal with drag as the object comes down. This is annoying to think about since I have no way of creating a 0G environment.

I just strongly feel the methods of testing this have so many variables that using a piece of wood that is unbalanced and using your hands to flip it is a non consistent variable to apply.

Edit 3: after rewatching the video twice more I discovered there are simulations. I don’t know how I missed that bit. I am satisfied with that. But I still want to test my theory. I also theorize that getting those conditions perfect will only render the object to spin perfectly a few times before flipping. Literally what the video explained now that I think about it. I’ll leave this comment here as a time stamp in brain fart history 🧠💨

author: @nkm6789
text:
Great video!

I'm partial to this (somewhat abstract) geometric explanation: 

In the rotating frame, write down the equations for conservation of energy and angular momentum, in terms of the three principal components of angular velocity. Each of those equations describes an ellipsoid in angular-velocity-space, and the angular velocity vector must lie on the intersection. Now think about how two concentric ellipsoids can intersect. 

If the energy is nearly as low as possible for a given momentum, (energy ellipsoid is small), they will intersect at two tiny circles along the axis for the largest moment of inertia. The angular velocity vector will precess around the tiny circles, barely changing. If energy is nearly as high as possible, they will intersect at two tiny circles along the axis for the smallest moment of inertia. But in the in-between case, the ellipsoids intersect at two large circles in a sort of double X shape, running all the way around the ellipsoids. The angular velocity moves along from one side of the ellipsoid to the other, which is a flip!

author: @Metrion77
text:
This seems like a very overcomplicated solution to a very simple problem. There's three relevant laws of dynamics.
1. There's no way to perfectly spin an object. There will always be a slight "pollutant" rotation energy on the other two axes.
2. Path of least resistance. Objects want to spin along the axis with the least inertia.
3. Gyroscopic force (ie heavy centripetal force along an axis, ie rotational inertia) resists rotational force on other axes.

author: @Artemetra
text:
I think you missed a major point here. The nut, nor the tennis racket don't get spun cleanly. 



Why does it seem stable for a moment, then goes unstable, and back? Would a nut swap ends if it were spun precisely? I mean, obviously there is perturbation in the spin, when it comes off the bolt or out of the hole, there is a component of force applied off-axis, (by the fingers) causing that little bit of instability which grows until it swaps ends. The system can't shake the instability, so it keeps swapping. Like when you spin a top - the more perfect you spin it, the longer it lasts and the less it wobbles. I believe if you could build a machine to get it spinning, and release it without any nudging, in a vacuum because a breath could add instability, it would NOT swap ends. 



You addressed instability, and you mention there's "virtually nothing you can do" to make the tennis racket not flip. But if you try enough times, it sometimes doesn't flip. I'm thinking those times are the more perfect throws.

author: @CarlosReche
text:
Hi, Derek! Thanks for bringing this subject to light.

Let me get this straight. There are infinite axis by which you can spin an object, right? When you mention the object's "three principal axis", I think there are only two that you can clearly point it out: the ones with the greatest and the smallest moments of inertia. All other axis of rotation (infinite possibilities) would result to be in an intermediate moment of inertia. Is that right?

So, here is a first conclusion: the stability of a rotating body is as higher as it is close to one of those two main axis (with greatest or smallest moments of inertia). Any rotation in an axis that has the slightest difference from these two would result to be an unstable one, and the body would flip after some time doing more or less spinnings (depending on how close it is to one of those stable axis).

Am I correct to think of it this way? And, if so, here is another thought: when spinning the tennis racket by hand, we problably cannot reach the precision to do it exactly in one of those two main axis. And maybe it would flip if we gave it enough time to make a lot of spinnings.

Which brings me to this: thinking the Earth as a body in constant changes (we have a lot of heat and pressure underneath our feet, evidenced by volcanic eruptions and earthquakes, not to mention external factors that might ruin any stable movement), what are the odds that our planet is really rotating in a stable axis? It makes me wonder, since there are a lot of cultures that talk about cataclysmic events that occur from time to time. And we have a bunch of evidences of it. But let's leave this conversation for another time. For now, for those who is interested (and also the skepticals), just reasearch about a book called "The Adam and Eve Story" and ask yourselves why it has been classified by the CIA in the 1960's, and has not been fully declassified by the Freedom of Information Act. I don't mean to be conspiratorial, but to me it's clear that this knowledge has been around for some time (like "always"), and is held by only a group of people.

author: @peterc.miller6087
text:
"The earth won't flip" claim is... interesting to me. As a firm believer in the Scientific Method, let me propose a hypothetical, backed by some degree of evidence, that maybe the earth *surface* is doing the flip, not the physical core or the magnetic field. I'm no conspiracy person - this idea just seems interesting to me, and it intuitively correct based on the little data we have about the Earth's internals, and I just want to share it.

First: We know *something* flips. It's either the Earth's surface or the Core, since we have evidence going back the on the ocean floors showing the magnetic fields changing:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geomagnetic_reversal

An eyebrow raising detail of the reversals is that they are random. 

Consider: The information provided in this video makes it very clear that a body whose mass changed from one axis to the other could induce the Dzhanibekov Effect.

Consider: The earths surface and upper mantle is mostly rigid, or at least certainly more rigid than the inner mantle, or at least more rigid than the fluid layer between the surface and the solid core.

Consider: It is reasonable to assume, and indeed we're getting recent data that in fact, plumes of mantle 'move around' inside the earth.
gradient

indicative
https://www.nasa.gov/feature/nasa-researchers-track-slowly-splitting-dent-in-earth-s-magnetic-field

Consider: A powerful plume or set of plumes arrive into the upper mantel *and cool and solidify and stick* to the upper mantle. Now you have an inner core which has not had a mass angular momentum change, but an the Earth's upper mantle and surface has. The inner core is mostly unperturbed, but the outer mantel and surface are now newly exposed to the Dzhanibekov Effect. The outer core has gone from rotationally spherical to rotationally tennis racket.

Could it be the outside surface of the earth is what flips during Geomagnetic reversal, rather than the core or the magnetism of the core? It kinda seems more reasonable that the surface flips, because I'm guessing the inner core of the earth is *way* more massive than the externals of the earth. 

Also the randomness of the planetary scale heat plumes - matching those of a lava lamp - would be random. Which is lovely since we even use lava lamps to help us generate truly random numbers:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1cUUfMeOijg

It seems much more likely that the core would be more stable and the lighter outer portions of the earth are what would be flipped.

Let's hypothesize for a moment and say it was the outer surface of the planet that flipped. (north to south) with the core remaining un altered. What evidence would there be to prove that on the surface of the earth? Obviously the magnetic record on the see floor - but that doesn't tell you want flipped, just that the magnetic field changed relative to the surface. 

What archaeological evidence would prove the *surface* flipped? For example, Is there any known archaeological method to know what direction the sun came from? Say like 'Hey at this time it was supposed to be winter here but it looks like it was summer, and stayed that way for a few thousand years'. No - that would be useless, because even if you could, it would be affected by seasons, but they're affected by seasons anyway - you'd have to have precision down to the year across a data set spanning millions. Too little signal to noise ratio no? You could be staring at the archeological showing sun direct data over cycling annual seasons, but you'd have no way of knowing if you were 6 months off.

I'm not currently aware of any method for humans to know aggregate periodic surface angles to the sun with that precision past the time of the most recent flip.

Does anyone else out there know of a way? Even one way will prove this idea wrong.

Sure... it is possible that it's the core flipping because *its* mass changes. But by what process? I'd like to hear an intuitive description of any core flipping or pole flipping, just like this video provided such an intuitive explanation of the Dzhanibekov Effect.

The plume process affecting the outer mass of the earth and cooling seems ... more intuitive and likely to me at the moment. 

Yay science!

author: @fla5183
text:
Interesting.  When I was taking my mechanical dynamics class 30+ years ago we discussed this as cross-coupling.  The example we used was a toy top or our class rings.  Spin either of them with the large end down and they will flip up.  Both are symmetric about 2 axes.  Both are also under 1g during the experiment.  Apparently it doesn't matter.  


It does explain why celestial bodies spin in a crazy way - over eons of time they have settled to the spin axis around maximum moment of inertia.

text: I'm 69, never heard of this effect, but, the Teacher here reminds me of my Science teacher in High school, back in 1965. Miss Hutchins, (I'm Australian),  was as far as we in her science classes were concerned, THE teacher of the century.   I was 15 back then, and like pretty much all the other teenage males, was utterly besotted by her. Glorious red hair, stunning eyes and smile,  a voice that effortlessly held the whole classes attention, and so much more. She was pretty much a 'natural' Teacher, and our Science exam results demonstrated that. Without exception, we would contrive to arrive early to every class, and do our damndest to delay the end of same. Till I die, miss Hutchins is the most memorable Teacher I ever knew, she taught much more than just Science, she taught us about the world, she  inspired us to want to see it,  to experience it, which I have, and continue to do. Thank you so much, Miss Hutchins,  Science teacher at Ashcroft High School, Green Valley, 1965, New South Wales Australia.

author: @SirPhysics
text:
The more I think about it, the less satisfying this explanation is. First, no explanation can be truly intuitive if it relies on fictitious forces (that's a bit like using gods to explain natural phenomenon). Second, it doesn't actually explain anything. Let's adopt the rotating reference frame used in the explanation so centrifugal forces are exerted on the small masses in the z-direction. You then automatically say that these centrifugal forces accelerate the masses away from the y-axis, but why do they do? Why are these centrifugal forces not balanced by the centripetal component of the tension forces the same way the centrifugal forces on the large masses are? You seem to be begging the question: assuming the masses will accelerate to explain why they accelerate.

I can simplify this explanation significantly (in an inertial reference frame, no less): if the small masses are displaced slightly, the tension forces within the disk are not strong enough to prevent them from moving farther out away from the axis of rotation. As the masses move farther away from the axis of rotation, the tension forces increase. Thus, the tension forces within the disk act as a restoring force, producing oscillatory motion*.

While this cleans up the explanation you provided and removes the need to rely on fictitious forces, it does not address the key issue of explaining WHY the tension forces aren't strong enough to prevent the masses from moving outwards. My best guess for why that is ultimately comes down to the fact that nothing is really preventing the disk from rotating along that axis so the tension forces don't need to be that strong. If you think about the large masses, if the tension forces holding them in place were not large enough to produce the required centripetal acceleration then the only way for the large masses to move outwards is for the entire disk to break apart. Therefore the tension forces exerted on the large masses are as strong as they need to be to keep them rotating at a constant distance from the axis up to the point of mechanical failure of the disk. For the smaller masses, this is not the case. They can be moved farther away from the axis of rotation without destroying the disk simply by rotating the disk. And so that's what happens. The reason the tension force on these masses is maximum once they have rotated 90 degrees is precisely because they have reached the point where they can no longer move farther away from the axis of rotation without destroying the disk, so like the big masses they have reached a point where the maximum tension force exerted on them is the mechanical limit of the disk.

It's not a perfect explanation, but it's as close as I'm going to get without spending more time on this than I'm willing to at the moment.

author: @modsmocs
text:
All good here, except his somewhat forced and self-comforting concluding remarks, without proof, that the Earth's current moment of inertia is sic. "stable" and unchanging.
"Stable" spinning orbits for a sphere in a vacuum is obviously dependent on the mass as well as the distribution of the mass within the sphere. The larger the mass the longer its "cycle" of "flipping". The tiny mass of the wing nut clearly shows in a cyclical frequency of only a few seconds. For a planetary size mass, such as the Earth, its quite possible the "flipping" cycle could be millions of years or more. Hence, "stable" is relative to the time-frame involved. It would still be "stable" from human civilizational perspective, if Earth's "flipping" frequency is say 10 million years each. The issue is we have no way to tell if the "flipping" is going to occur the day after tomorrow (pun intended) or a million years later; or if global climate change might melt the ice-caps and lead to accelerated change to the planet's moment of inertia due to changing mass distribution.

Many brilliant physicists/geologists/archaeologists probably knows it and have come to the same dreadful conclusion, which is perhaps why Dr. Terry Tao avoided being interviewed. If I were Terry, I'd do the same by giving some excuse to side-step the issue publicly, so I avoid being labelled as an alarmist. And besides, even if my secret Supercomputer calculations shows Earth is definitively gonna "flip" in 5 years time, or 50,000 years time, there's really nothing we could do about it. We could nuke an incoming big-ass asteroid and stop if from wiping out humanity, but there's really nothing much we can do about a physical law of angular momentum, yeah?

Perhaps, this could explain the Fermi Paradox of why our Universe is so damn quiet (i.e. lacking in Alien civilizational signatures)....because most of them/us gets "rebooted" on a global scale quite regularly simply due to the laws of nature.

author: @effyleven
text:
I discovered a different but equally odd rotary phenomenon when I was a nine year old kid in school. 
That was back in the days of dip pens, and all school desks had a ceramic inkwell in the top right-hand corner. Those inkwells were a LOOSE FIT in their wooden sockets, so they could be slipped out for filling with ink. Also, a kid could jiggle them about to make a REALLY annoying noise, ìf he had a mind to.
Well, I found that if I put my index finger in the hole in the middle of a dry inkwell, and then moved that inkwell around against its socket in a circular path...

... NOT twisting my finger, you understand, but moving it in a circular orbit, like driving a disc round on a turntable...

... then something very odd happened.

As I moved my finger in a circle - pressing the inkwell against the side wall of its socket - the 'well immediately began a rotation of its OWN. It twisted itself around on my finger, *BUT IN THE WRONG DIRECTION, AND AT ABOUT 1/5th MY CIRCLING SPEED.*

Yes. If I moved my finger in a circular orbit clockwise, the inkwell would bear against its socket and twist around on my finger, (and in the socket) in an anti-clockwise direction, but slowly... as I remember about 1/5 or 1/4  speed. Anyway, it was certainly always the same proportion of my circular rotating rate. 
If I reversed my direction of circling, the inkwell also immediately reversed and went the other way, at that same slower speed.

I hope I have explained this strange behaviour in terms that can be understood.

I have never met anybody who ever noticed this, or gave other examples of it, let alone being able to explain it to me. Even when the Internet came along, I never (not yet) found any reference to it... unless YOU know better, of course.
If you have been, thanks for reading... and if you can add anything to my strange "discovery," please do.

author: @Dzeno2010
text:
Hey Derek and rest of the internet,

It's an awesome video but I run into 1 problem with YOUR explanation (not the mathematical one).
An good, intuitive and logical explanation has to be self-consistent.
That is precisely where I run into a problem, let me explain:
For reference I'm going to use the example you explain at @7:10
So as you state we have the 1st axis or x-axis; 2nd axis or y-axis; and 3rd axis or z-axis.
This goes from lowest to highest moment of inertia.

Now your explanation is completely logical and understandable when we rotate around the 2nd axis.
However I would then expect the same to happen to the first axis as this should result in a similar result if we assume that no object "realistically" ever spins perfectly around it's axis. So in that case the heavy point masses would also be tilted slightly off-axis and would in turn also result in a "flipping" effect similar to how it does around the 2nd axis. The main difference in that case would be that the period/frequency of flipping would just be different, maybe an other of magnitude, but it wouldn't make it 0 nor infinity.

Don't get me wrong I really love to have an intuitive explanation but it needs to then be self-consistent.
Maybe I'm missing something or overlooking something, that's possible. However after discussing it with a friend of mine we both came to the same conclusion.

Hopefully I missed something but otherwise the explanation needs adjusting and unfortunately we still won't have an intuitive/logical explanation (which is the main point of the video).

Best regards,
A fervent watcher of your interesting educational videos

author: @epictales1307
text:
I had seen a video on this subject awhile back and today I heard something about it and it turned on the lightbulb. I searched for a video of this happening and wrote in the comments:

"I know why they do this, and it is a lot more simple than it looks!! To figure it out, all you have to do it watch the ending half of this when the handle spins slowly enough that you can play in slow motion  and see the moment of change. author: @markhenderson7930
text:
I watched this video (or at least part of it) about 6 months ago.  This morning I started jotting down some ideas I had related to this concept.  I started watching this video again while writing so I could get the terminology correct.  I'm going to copy and paste what I wrote here and would love any feedback:  

Intermediate Axis Theory to explain the Earth’s reversing magnetic field.

The following are my thoughts, inspired by the YouTube video on the channel Veritasium entitled The Vizarre Behavior of Rotating Bodies.  The video describes the 3 principal axes of a mass: 1st axis has the smallest moment of inertia, 3rd axis has the greatest moment of inertia, and the 2nd axis (the intermediate axis) has a moment of inertia between the first and second.

Imagine the Earth’s inner solid core is spinning in what essentially is zero gravity.  Because the mass of the earth somewhat equally surrounds the inner core, the gravitational forces cancel each other out.  The inner core is surrounded by (suspended in) the liquid core.  Suppose the inner core in not perfectly spherical and has an intermediate axis.

The intermediate axis theorem, based on The Twisting Tennis Racket as published in the Journal of Dynamics and Differential Equations, Vol. 3. No. 1, 1991 and possibly the Russian discovery by Cosmonaut Vladimir Dzhanibekov while removing a wingnut in a failing space station, is summarized as follows:  A body that has 3 unequal axes will spin with stability about its first and third axes, but spinning about its second, or intermediate axis, will cause it to flip 180 degrees about that intermediate axis.

I don’t have the physics knowledge to explain any of this, these are just my thoughts.

My Theory is that the inner core of the Earth has 3 unequal axes and is spinning about its intermediate axis.

I remember some things from my days at Purdue University, many years ago, and one of those things is the involvement of the Reynolds number when scaling experiments.  That is really the total of what I remember about the Reynolds number, but basically it means that I have no way to contemplate modeling this.

I am re-watching the above-referenced video and I either didn’t watch it long enough the first time (about  months ago) or blocked out a part of the video, but the part that just played is explaining what I just started trying to explain.  I just finished the video, and there is a difference between what I started to write and what was just explained.  The video concluded that the Earth as a whole must be spinning about it’s 3rd axis, but didn’t consider the solid inner core within the liquid outer core.

author: @Jefferylibido
text:
The tennis racquet theorem is pretty interesting, it shows exactly why pro players serve the way they do:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uyTtZE6LSek (play at x0.25 speed)

Notice how they swing the racquet face-closed (almost like trying to hit the ball with the frame) and only open up the racquet face at the very last milliseconds before they hit the ball. 

This seems counterintuitive at first but as this video explains, with the racquet face closed, it rotates along the "3rd axis" with the maximum angular inertia and greater stableness, therefore generating maximum power, whereas an open racquet face means it rotates along the least stable second axis with less angular inertia, therefore unable to generate sufficient power.

author: @sakinano99
text:
the graphic at 10:10 is incorrect, the direction of the earth's rotation should stay the same, due to conservation of angular momentum. if you watch closely, the wingnut flips orientation, but it's direction of spin is always the same, which has to be true since angular momentum must be conserved.


an interesting implication of this is that in the process of flipping over, there is a fraction of a second where the wingnut actually stops spinning around it's intermediate axis before it begins spinning around the intermediate axis in the opposite direction (which produces the same angular momentum since the orientation has flipped too). a similar effect can be observed in slow motion footage of a tippe top

It was my understanding that centrifugal force was a bit of a misnomer.

My understanding was that there was really no such centrifugal force pushing masses away from an axis of rotation, each mass simply wants to maintain is vector motion perpendicular to the face of the disc.
The centripetal force being the force that pulls it around into circular motion toward said axis of rotation (the center of the disc).

If one of these masses were to detach, it would fly out on a trajectory perpendicular to the face of the disc at the moment of release, instead of directly away from the edge (centrifugal).

Is this understanding incorrect?

author: @daffidavit
text: In aviation, the three-axis of an airplane are similar to the axis of the tennis racket.    The first is the "longitudinal axis" which runs from the nose of the airplane to the tail.  The second axis is known as the "Lateral Axis" which runs from one wingtip to the other wingtip.  The third is the "vertical axis" which runs from the roof of the cabin through the bottom.  The rotations of the airplane about the three-axis are known as "roll", "pitch" and "yaw" respectively.  I'd be interested in seeing if an aerobatic airplane with super-strong wings would react the same way as the tennis racket if the pilot were able to "snap" the airplane into an immediate "accelerated stall" in the pitch-up attitude and see if it performs similarly to the tennis racket.

There is an (pretty important) issue I have not been able to solve for myself, though I admit I have not been working much on it. I hope someone will explain it to me.


The intuitive and animated way of looking at the problem tells us that, in the non inertial reference frame in which all of the four masses are initially at rest, the big masses are in unstable equilibrium (a small initial angle of rotation about the x-axis is magnified).


Why wouldn't this same argument work is we swapped the roles of the small masses vs the big masses (in other words, if we rotated the disc about the x axis instead of the y axis)? Why is rotation about the intermediate moment of inertia axis unstable, despite being stable about the minimum moment of inertia one?

author: @underdegunn1174
text:
Unfortunately, the examples of asteroids and Mars you used in the end sequence where you said the world is not going to flip over were not valid in that they, although seeming similar, are completely different from the Earth.  Neither have a magnetic field because their cores are dead. Magnetic fields are ACTIVE. You did mention gravity but gravity is not dynamic. It is static.  And, being static, those examples will only change their states according to Newton's 3rd. 
Magnetism, on the other hand, is dynamic - it is electromechanical. The Earth is both a dynamo and a battery - one with a fluid core. That fluid core (liquid iron we are told) can be compressed or stretched by the pull of the poles. It can also have different areas of it's "electrolyte" be at a different specific gravity depending on the state of charge in different areas. (Cheap car battery cell tester anyone?) With this in mind, imagine the poles shifting 90 degrees and holding there for a period of time. What happens when the magnetic fields, now at 90 degrees to the "normal," cause the fluid core to change it's state - either electrically or physically or probably both?  Either to cause a bulge in the Y-Axis due to the new magnetic pull, or the specific gravity of the electrolyte (the same fluid core) to change (lighter or heavier) in certain areas as related to it's charge and position to that of the Y-Axis? 
And don't forget the natural Y-Axis wobble that is always present. 
The earth is not static - as was almost every example you presented except the water bottle. 
The earth is, in every respect, very very very active. And, anything active can change of itself.
Think on it.  
Cheers.

author: @choodooloo8
text:
Before I even reach the halfway point of this video, here's my hypothesis:


Of the rotating handle with 90-degree lever arm at the center, there are two forces at play: 


First lets assume the rotating body is rotating on the X-Axis and that the origin is at the midsection of the long section of the handle.
Lets call the rotating body a "handle"
Lets call the long section of the handle "Side A"
Lets call the smaller and orthogonal section of the handle "Side B"  


The object is spun about the X-Axis which cuts through the center of Side B and Side A.


In an Ideal environment
1. Side A will continue to rotate about the X-Axis due to it's angular momentum unless disturbed by another force.
2. Side B will also continue to rotate about the X-Axis due to it's angular momentum unless disturbed by another force.


Realistically however, it is impossible to have the X-Axis cut perfectly down the center of Side B and Side A. All it takes is for the axis to be off by less than a micron for turbulence to ensue.


Because of this; Side B is not in perfect rotation and will eventually accelerate away from the X-Axis once it has built enough angular momentum to overcome the angular momentum of Side A.


Once this Side B angular moment overpowers Side A angular momentum, Side B will cross over to the other side of the X-Axis.


At this point, the angular momentum of Side B will have momentarily dissipated and the angular momentum of Side A will once again take over the system and then the phenomenon will continue as if in a never ending loop, unless the handle is acted upon by another force.

author: @gdeangelkick
text:
Here is the intuitive explanation for the flip:  it is the the sum of a remainder of  an unbalanced equation inherent to the mathematical model of the spin NEARLY around 2nd axis but not precisely.  
You missed a piece of the puzzle about the Earth flipping.  You assume no exogenous energy added into the system to compensate for the dissipation of kenetic energy into heat, etc. that happens when spinning the bottle of liquid solution in space.  Introduce a laser to heat the water inside the bottle, and see if it flips.  However, the evidence of magnetic poles reversing comes from the frame of reference of the Earth itself (magma rock formation), so if what occurred was a complete flip of the earth, it would not affect the polarity of magma-iron laid down on the Earth.  The frame of reference (iron heavy compass "North") and the object (the magma flows) would remain unaltered. However, if you consider that the earth may have a core with different moments or inertia than it's crust, AND that the layer in between is a fluid layer which is not homogeneous in mass-density, etc., it is certainly possible to construct a model where the core flips inside the Earth! 

This theory would be supported if the magnetic pole reversals occurred during the span of a constant number or rotations. So if you zoom in on every flip, and model the amount of magma spread in a single day, you would find the time to effect a magentic reversal would be relatively constant.  (I revised this part after watching more closely some of the slo-mo videos of stuff, but someone who has actually dealt with the math of astral bodies can reply to this comment with what the duration of the flip would be a function of).

* Well there is a bug in youtube's comment parser, because I made this post exactly once, but it's showing up multiple times. This is a test to see if they are all displaying the same comment record, or are duplicate records.  LOL... good job Alphabet

author: @MarcusAndersonsBlog
text:
[Comment updated&reposted 8Oct)]The fluid dynamics of the earth's liquid outer core and massive solid inner core have natural stability yes, but can be forced into an unstable response by an external disturbance. That disturbance could be nothing more than a small 26000 year wobble, which the Earth just so happens to have. If this wobble is spiraling (to or away from its wobble-pole) then it could well result in a slow flip of the earth over in a just few more wobble cycles (50,000 years). 

This is not just a likely scenario. Almost every large object in our solar system revolves and rotates in an anti-clockwise direction. Uranus however has an almost horizontal (98°) inclination off the vertical making it either a rare clockwise rotation or inverted and just over half way through an inversion cycle. Venus is completely retrograde (clockwise) suggesting it has re-stabilised in the inverted state. Pluto sits at 120° or 60° retrograde. All the other planets have about 25° wobble except Jupiter at 3°, and only Mercury has no significant wobble (0°).

Planetary inversion is a real phenomenon, that can happen in at least the extreme circumstances demonstrated by the Russian cosmonaut. There is certainly not yet enough evidence to claim that this can not happen, or has not happened, to the earth. So why would anyone assert that it hasn't or cant, if the truth is that nobody actually knows? That position amounts to a belief, not science, and because of the gravity of the situation, I prefer to believe it can happen. Show me some better evidence than a specious 1980's theory offered up by the propagandist Russian government.

Another narration from the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him):

"When three things appear, no soul will benefit from faith if it had not previously believed or had not earned good through its faith: the rising of the sun from the west, the Dajjal (the Antichrist), and the Beast of the earth." (Jami' at-Tirmidhi 3072)

3. Sunan Ibn Majah
The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) said:

"The Hour will not come until the sun rises from the west. When it rises and the people see it, they will all believe, but that will be the time when no good will it do to a soul to believe then, if it believed not before." (Sunan Ibn Majah 4076)

4. Musnad Ahmad
Another hadith states:

"The Hour will not be established until the sun rises from the west. When it rises from the west and the people see it, then all of them will believe. That is the time when it will not benefit a soul to believe then if it did not believe before." (Musnad Ahmad 27182)

Here is my Layman's Way of explaining the Dzhanibekov Effect:  
(1) When an object is rotated along its least stable axis (of rotation), 
(2) it tends to "flip" along its most stable axis, 
(3) and it will "flip" in the opposite direction of any wobble it has along the remaining axis.

Great video (sincerely) . . . but please consider the above.

author: @vladstavrou5730
text:
I don't find this explanation complete. Theorists fall face-deep into their theories without first accounting for the object imperfections, the throw or spin balance inconsistency, air density or gravity effects. 
 
When he throws the tennis racket, he himself introduces the spin effect he's so excited by which is simply enhanced by the imbalanced object. Same with the rest of the examples. Hand-spins are so flawed that the spinned objects take less time to flip while the wing-nut example takes longer because it starts a more stable rotation from the screw that it's on. Of course nuts and screws still have small gaps between them and so it's still not a perfect rotation and flips in the end. 
 
This whole thing reminds me of the Fibonacci sequence where a small imperfection in the rotation slowly gets out of hand but resets after the flip on the other side. If we take some same-shaped objects shown here and spin them with a machine in perfect conditions I don't believe they're ever flip. 

This is a man-introduced effect, not a physics law.

text: To me the explanation is that when you perform a twist, you can’t apply exact force parallel to that axis, ergo, after each rotation, inertia accumulate and must be discharged with the flip, then accumulating again.. that force cannot be discharged linearly because the force on the other axis is much greater, inducing a lot of gyroscopic inertia, which is bypassed when, after few (constant) rotations the force is strong enough to perform the flip.. if you have an exactly distributed force, you won’t have the flip.. or at least it will come every few years

Hi Derek, thank you for the wonderful and high quality videos you have made these years. I watched many of them if not all. It is a great relieve that Dzhanibekov effect will not flip the earth.  
I am an engineer with experties on vehicle (aircrafts, ships and cars) dynamics and stabilities. I have a new way to understand Dzhanibekov effect. I am planning to make a video on this issue. If possible for education purpose, I would like to ask your permission to use the part of your video from 1:06 to 1:36 which is a perfect description for this phenomenon. The root cause for Dzhanibekov effect is the nonlinear inertial torques (the angular velocity vector cross product the angular momentum). Recently, I made a scietific discovery that the same nonlinear inertial torques (moment) have caused many aircraft crashes, ship capsizes, and car rollovers. I published my findings in the book,  Nonlinear Instability and Inertial Coupling effects (2018). However, these inertial torques have been neglected in all these engieering fields so far. To save lives, I think that education for this phenoemnon is important. Recently, I posted two videos: The Physics of Golden Ray capsize, parts 1 and 2 on YouTube for your reference. I appreciate if you can allow me to use the part of your wonderful video above. 
Steve Shaojie Tang

author: @Stardust_Ziggy
text: With all due respect, the video does not intuitively explain the effect. Why? The same reasoning could be applied if we rotated the disc about the x-axis, but this doesn't work. You address this problem in the description using energy principles, which again might be not intuitive for everyone and is a mathematical approach. I highly suggest reading Taos post, in which he argues with the Coriolis force. For those mathematically gifted, I still think the easiest explanation is based on stability analysis (and geometric reasoning). The best I encountered so far is in chapter 15 of "Introduction to Mechanics and Symmetry" by Jerrold Marsden and Tudor Ratiu.

This effect evidently shows, that wherever we have rotation, its spin is kept not by the material object, but by the ETHER-whirl.

The reason is gravitational forces Imagine the spinning nut having a perfect circlular globe tightly all around it  let's call that it's existential perimeter. When spinning along the axis you will find that the part of the globe where the bottom of the nut spins has less mass compared to the top side which fills the globe more completely. We'll gravity has a tendency to even this imbalanceout by flipping the object. It will keep doing this in order to distribute the mass evenly within the globe. This is why planets are round not square. Nature will always stick to the globe shape as it most ideally distributes mass within it compared to a square where the corners are further away from the center. That is a theoretic explanation. Let's just say science has it all wrong. Gravity is not a force in itself. It is a manifestation of various natural laws

author: @GregHart2880
text:
Well let's see. In free fall on a space station,  what we observe,  while in the space station,  is limited by its size, so, the full effect cannot be seen. Neither can it be seen inside one of those close to free fall plane rides.

What this wing nut is, obviously,  proving,  is simple. When you undo a normal screw, wing nut, bottle top, it's anti clockwise. Clockwise to do it back up again. When the wing nut was unscrewed and alliwed to free fall continue its flight, it came off spinning anti clockwise. As it proceeded across the room, in its own imperfect way, it was doing what a gyroscope does on Earth. Consider a spinning gyroscope.  You can put it on a table then pull the top down to a little above flat and let it go. With the spinning wheel oart spinning over the edge of the table, hitting nothing,  the gyroscope remains as is until interfered with or rotation drops off enough. The only thing anchoring it to the table is the small bit upon which it was standing, down the centre of its pole. While spinning off at an angle, push the top of it down far enough and it drops off to the floor and keeps spinning but if you started the gyroscope spinning clockwise (right handed people normally do) and then caused it to fall by pushing the top down too far off the table, when it hits the floor,  from your perspective looking down at it, it's now spinning anti clockwise. It, also, kanded with a heavier thud than if you had the same gyroscope, not spinning and dropped it on its point from the same height.

If the gyroscope is a perfect one, then because of the spin, you can see the rest of its weight doesn't matter, in the tests above until the top and bottom points are almist or actually the same height. The original point,  on the table, gas lost enough spin point energy, diwn its shaft, to make it drop.

So, with that in mind, remember a wing nut is an imperfect simulacrum of a gyroscope but in free fall has advantages. Whwn it unscrews and flies off, it is building a minor spin point like a gyroscope does. Unfortunately,  the only gyroscopes I have observed,  have been on Earth. In space, the leading point of the spin shaft creates a spin point that would be happy not to flip over but unfortunately, the space station is orbiting Earth which is, itself,  spinning.  You may think, in free fall, on a space station,  that there's no gravity. OK so what's holding the space ship in orbit so it doesn't shoot off into space? The effects of the soace station orbiting and Earth spinning,  is enough to create something best visualised by looking from galactic north, outside our gakaxy, back down at it. See the arms? Earth spins mini versions of that, too. The wing nut with its spin point bubble hits a fronf like point of gravity emanating from Earth,  is not powerful enough to bash through it and because this point of gravity is bent like the arms of a galaxy, the spin point gravity bobs down minutely enough to flip the wing nut over and then cause drag so that the wing nut doesn't revolve but apoears ro just flip and continue. Now the wing nut, originally spinning anti clockwise,  is now spinning clockwise and cancels out the mini gravitic effect inside the tube and vegins making another at the leading edge if the spin. It hits another gravitic frond, flips the same and on it goes.

What I would like to see is the same wing nut released facing upwards or downwards (relatuve to Earth). It will hit a gravitic frond eventually but not likely to flip and repeat at the same rate as horizontally. 

Simple, isn't it? BTW, that gyroscope on Earth. Make it out of superconductor material, super freeze it and spin it on top of a neodymium magnet for an interesting effect you may not have witnessed before.

author: @themoviemaniac8416
text: First, an object does not have merely three axes (the plural form of axis). It actually has an infinite number. It's our own abstraction of space that limits us to think of three. Any object made to spin on an "axis" where the weight is distributed evenly on that axis will spin without odd behavior causing flipping or turning on another axis. It's practically impossible to get such a spin continuously for a long period of time as there will always be a little more weight on one side vs the other. The actually change in spin or motion on another axis however will be almost imperceptible in this situation. In the matter of the Wing Nut spinning through weightless space, then suddenly flipping 180 degrees and continuing to spin, then doing it again, has to do with that almost imperceptible difference in weight. The weight of the nut is greater on the wing side vs the nut side. The axis of those two sides takes a long time to reach a "tipping point" due to the inertial motion of spinning on the other axis, but when it does, the spin on that axis takes place suddenly and then hesitates until the next time the tipping point is reached. It's really not all that hard to understand.

author: @vitalstatistic63
text:
OK. I'm at 6:20, he's planning to prove Richard Feynman wrong, and find an intuitive reason. The long T shaped handle has just been unscrewed and spun from the panel in the Space Station and flipped it's long axis several times.

Here it is...
It's been established that an object's spin will be stable if it spins on the long axis or the short axis, but will flip as it spins on it's medium axis. The reason, as the object spins around the shaft with the T crossbar handle rotating, if the shaft goes minutely off centre, centrifugal force will throw it outwards and away from the centre of rotation until it is perpendicular to the direction of spin, at this time inertia will cause the already moving shaft to continue away from it's original direction and flip to the other end where it will settle momentarily due to running out of inertia. A short time later the shaft will be thrown off centre again and flip back to it's original direction, where the process will repeat.

Now, carry on and see if that's what the video finds...

author: @aardque
text:
Took it on a Zero G plane! It must have been an affront to the crew. 
"What, are we practicing pizza delivery now?" 
"No, it's a science experiment." 
"A plywood Frisbee with a hole cut out of it is a science experiment, fit for Zero G? You couldn't have at least made it out of carbon fiber, or titanium?"
"You know what, I might plug Zero G in the video I make from this, but it sounds like they don't actually need it..."


Now I will interpret the "Tennis Racket" effect. 

I think the confusion comes from trying to express the events as "angular momentums," which I understand to be paths through which energy dissipates. However, we know a rotating object does so indefinitely, so if energy dissipates, it is also replaced.

You must be familiar with the "Crookes radiometer." It does not store, nor dissipate energy, yet it spins indefinitely in the presence of radiation. The tennis racket is interacting with the stream of time, in the same way a physical body encounters gravity, near a larger body. 

As the high moment of inertia axis pierces the time stream, all areas are equally affected, but the low moment of inertia axis gets "frame dragged," by the passage of time, which robs inertia from the high moment axis since it must drag the low axis along, or disintegrate, until finally an inertial tipping point is reached and the equation resets.

In fact, one could probably arrange these devices to provide a physical representation of the orientation and direction of the stream of time, a "Kellers chronometron," if you will, since chronometer is already taken and arguably, more mundane a term.

author: @oldjoec3710
text: Belated comment:  Notice in the videos of each real object that the final spin direction and spin axis of the object always continue to be the same as the initial conditions, when seen in relation to our external fixed inertial frame.  So, even if this did happen to a roughly spherical planet, that planet's spin would continue to align with all the other planets, but its topographical features (continents, etc.) would invert from north to south.  We would not see Venus as spinning backwards or Uranus as spinning sideways, and Earth would definitely NOT spin clockwise in relation to solar north, as shown in the illustration at 10:13.  Of course, on earth's surface we would reverse from west-to-east motion as north and south became reversed, a delta-V of up to 3,300 KpH.  This could be something of a wild ride, depending on how fast it happened.

author: @mikewatson1105
text:
This is just a question, not a solution or a proof. This looks to me like a liberated, or unrestrained version of the twisting forces on the blades of a variable pitch aircraft propeller in flight. In a single engine aircraft, the control mechanism drives the blades towards coarse pitch and the centripetal forces created by the engine driven spin drives the blades towards fine pitch. This is because the weight in the leading and trailing edges are a bir farther away from the axis of rotation in this condition. The rotation towards fine pitch is of course limited by the fine pitch stop, but if the fine pitch stop didn’t exist, the blade pitch should oscillate between equal and opposite positive and negative blade angles if I disregard friction and aerodynamic effects. Blade angle is controlled by engine oil pressure, so if the oil pressure fails, the centrifugal blade twisting moment will twist the blades to fine,allowing the aircraft airspeed to spin up the engine rpm, helping the pilot to ‘bump start’ the engine. In a multi engine or a single engine aerobatic aircraft, there is a counterweight at right angles to the plane of the propeller blade so if there is a loss of oil pressure, the blades go to the feather position and the engine can stop, not being driven by the airspeed through the propeller.
So, is a variable pitch propeller a restrained version of this multi-axis spinning effect? I look forward to your thoughts, thank you.

author: @8cto289
text:
I feel like this theory may *partially* explain the reason Earth seems to wobble on its rotating axis very slowly. Even though it is spinning on the axis with the greatest moment of inertia, like everything in the universe, it is not perfect. The fact that Earth’s surface doesn’t have uniform mass, along with the fact crust and magma are constantly shifting and flowing may be enough to move the axis with the greatest moment of inertia. The earth’s rotational axis follows suit. I know there is probably holes in my logic here, and, as I said in the beginning, I am not stating that this is the absolute/ a major factor in why the earth wobbles on its axis.

Edit: I looked up why the earth wobbles on its axis, and people said this before me, with extra details that I didn’t think of.

author: @Smachenspiel
text: The author of this video makes a very subtle error.  For example, the disc doesn't necessarily go "back-and-forth" (ref. 9:26).  Think of a rigid-bar pendulum being placed near the top of it's swing arc and RELEASED, vs. the same pendulum being placed near the top and PUSHED.  The released one goes "back-and-forth" but the latter can go through the peak repeatedly.  The animation at 9:20 shows an example of the latter, while the video author states the former.

author: @kdmdlo
text:
NOOOOO!  At 7:31 you mention "centripetal forces".  THERE IS NO SUCH THING!!!  Centripetal terms are kinematic effects; they arise from the acceleration expressions (omega x [omega x X]) and not from the force side of Newton2.  And at 7:44 you mention "centrifugal forces".  Dear Lord.  You are my worst enemy!  I just finished teaching a first year graduate course in 3d, particle and rigid body dynamics (in which we dealt extensively with multiple rotating reference frames) and you're casually - and incorrectly - talking about things these things, further sowing long-term confusion rather than elucidating facts about basic physics.  Oh, my achin' back!  

By the way, a simple description of Euler's equations doesn't require any high-end mathematics (once you've taken all of the necessary derivatives) ... and can be used to explain what's going on.  Don't shy away from math.  It's our friend (and it has the added bonus of providing a way to quantify what we see).

author: @bobbie9090
text: "So the earth won't flip - its spinning about the axis with the maximum moment of inertia".  That sounds like a simplied mathematicians explanation and didn't provide any evidence the earth can't flip. The earth is not an asteroid. It is a very dynamic elastic system with a molten core that has surges of molten movement, and plate tectonics that cause large bodies far from the center to migrate. Then there are shorter term effects such as the earth redepositing large amounts of moisture such as glaciers aroind the planet.  For example the polar ice sheet is currently  3km thick sure not much compared to the 12000km diameter of the earth but the point is the earth look relatively static in our human viewframe but it is far from static. Look at even the 3 gorges dam filling creating a momentary imbalance.  So I don't think a simple mathematical equation is even close the proving the earth can't flip.  Maybe we should try modeling more dramatic scenarios that prove that the earth actually could flip such as adding larger amounts of polar ice or extreme mountain buildup with plate tectonics causing 100,000m high mountains. So many variables to test. It may help explain a lot of things that happen on earth such as the geological time periods Cretaceous, Jurassic,  Triassic, Permian etc which almost occur with approximate regulariy. I shall make it my ambition to prove you wrong that the earth can't mathematically flip. Your math may be right at a snapshot in time but far too simple for something as complex as earth.

text: Where would we obtain our massless disc, and what is it made of?  As for point masses, let's assume they are made of Osmium, which is very heavy. Intuitively I feel that centrifugal forces would be created, and unless they were firmly attached to your disc the heavy masses would fly away from the disc in opposite directions. author: @johnnemo4146
text:
What I found fascinating was that Richard Feynman considered it impossible to solve this intuitively.
That tells you a lot about quantum physicists, for whom mathematics IS reality (and make errors because they think that everything possible in the mathematics they use in their models, is also possible in reality).
In fact, the thing is quite simple, and you don't have to be a mathematician to solve it, and although I was too lazy to stop the video and analyze the thing completely, the solution was immediately sketched in my mind as something caused by minuscule imperfections causing amplifying forces. From that point, all there was to do would have been to make a complete intuitive forces analysis, like in the video.

But while the tennis racket does the flip in just one turn around the first axis of (lowest) inertia, when rotated around the intermediate (second) axis of inertia, it would seem intuitively logical for me that even if you rotate it long enough around the lowest inertia axis (in space), a flip should eventually happen because it doesn't rotate around the axis of maximum inertia (third).

And now, as I type,  I just I remembered the T-shaped object on 5:16, which rotates on its first axis and flips, and understood that the wing nut also rotates around the first, not the second one, like it does in the example of the tennis racket axis . The greatest the mass asymmetry along the first axis, the quicker the flip happens (I guess) when rotated around the first axis.
So mass asymmetric objects along the first axis, rotating around the first axis should flip, but the flip happens faster when rotating around the second axis (tennis racket example). 

It would be interesting to see a tennis racket rotated in space around its second axis. Would it flip at each turn? Probably, but if rotated around its first axis, it would flip just like the T-shaped object or the wing nut.

In conclusion the intermediate axis theory and the Dzhanibekov effect are not exactly the same thing, because the wing nut and the T-shaped object did not rotate around the second, intermediate axis, but the first one.
One could push the thing further, but I'm hungry now...

author: @QDWhite
text:
Shouldn't rotation around the primary axis be subject to the same instability according to this explanation? Seems like something's still missing, no?

Edit: I found a math overflow explanation directly from Terry Tao that answers this exactly. To sum up, the Coriolis force on the large masses when rotating around the primary axis are significant enough to keep the spinning constrained to the primary axis.  The Coriolis forces are negligible when spinning around the intermediate axis, so it flips.

TL;DR the Coriolis effect counteracts the centrigual effect when spinning on the primary axis. Around the intermediate axis, the Coriolis effect is not enough to prevent the flipping.

author: @rtmirand
text: Great explanation, you succeeded. To be slightly more precise, the earth is an oblate spheroid, and for that reason it is spinning stably -  like a pancake  - around the axis through the pancake's center and perpendicular to the plane of the pancake. I think that Feynman would have come up with the same explanation but he probably would have needed more time to compose a proper account than he was willing to spend during the filming of his lecture.  Finally, some posts say that if you remove any imperfections and the initial slight tilt, then disk should spin without flipping; it would in an IDEAL, imaginary situation, but this particular intermediate moment of inertia case is characterized by what is known as an "unstable equilibrium" in physics. Since there are always imperfections in the real world, the unstable equilibrium will lead to your disk flipping.

author: @danvladoiu9365
text: Since angular momentum has to be conserved (for no external torque) even a solid object spinning about either of its two principal axes, i.e. with the smallest and intermediate moments of inertia I1 and I2, will eventually flip and spin about its third principal axis with the largest moment of inertia I3 due to disipative forces within the object which will cause its kinetic energy to dampen over time (K = L^2 /2 x I3). Liquid filled objects do magnify an effect which is otherwise always present.

Think about drawing a "center-line" through the center of gravity of an object on each side to form the three axes. The rule goes like this: "You cannot flip something over an axis where the edge that's flipping is further away from its center-line than the edge that is not flipping [ given that there exists a non-flipping edge that is closer to its center-line ]".

author: @DeepFriedOreoOffline
text:
That experiment with the bottle of water actually proves a lot about why gravity on earth works:

At the start, they spin the bottle sideways and it twists like a ballerina. After a few seconds it starts to become unstable, and it shifts to the state of least resistance. If you look carefully when it does that, the axis it started spinning on is technically no longer actually spinning! The inertia from the spin is a part of the final rotational movement, but because that movement is extended over the whole object, rather than the object continuing to spin around that axis at the same rate, the spin is altered in the same way that the forces that caused the spin shift to ease the rotation of the object... It's actually amazing to see when you understand what's happening!

text:
I have two questions about this, and maybe you, or somebody else reading these comments, would be able to answer them.

1. Out of curiosity, hypothetically, if the planet did flip, given the information you've explained, and the time it takes to complete one rotation along the normal axis of rotation (one day, of course.) how long would it take to flip?

2. What changes to the inertial points of the earth would occur if, let's somewhat less hypothetically say, we go from having a large mass of ice at each end to having a landmass at one end and ocean at the other with the ice distributed over the planet in the form of water?

I'll be honest, I'd love to tease out these questions myself, but I'm notoriously incompetent with higher math, and whatever I came up with would probably not be reliably accurate, even if I did manage to find an answer, so I thought I'd throw this out and see if anybody is more up to the task than I am.

Is this Copernicus coming back to haunt us? That's crazy. We're looking out at the whole universe. author: @BoltonnStrid
text:
The explanation of why the containers stop rotating about the main axis is wrong.
The real reason is that the liquid inside goes to the POLES, catalizing the mass there, and thus reducing the weight on the other axis.

So why a rocket does not invert its spin axis, while a bolt does?
because the bolt has a larger base than the axis.
All the examples show this.
Geometry of the object is important here.
A rocket is thin and long, thus the secondary axes of rotation are ininfluent: their centrifugal force is nearly null in relation to the mass.
If you would add enough rotation though, it could invert its spin.

A sphere will never invert its spin axis.
The reason is easy: there is no secondary axis.
A disc example is the same.

Also, resonance is another factor to put in that was not considered at all in the video.
Especially for small objects, resonance is a major factor that would push the object to invert its axis of rotation by cumulating power on the secondary axis at each roll, when the conditions are met.

He doesn’t address why a perturbation in rotations about other axes doesn’t cause this phenomenon. It’s too hard to briefly explain in text, but the result is that you get precession, and variation of obliquity, which is what two of the milankovitch cycles are. The sun and the moon are the source of the nudges that perturb the earth, but you could remove them and it would continue to process and vary obliquity for some time until energy is dissipated like he discusses later on, just like how the T handle flips for quite a while. The reason that precession occurs and not a complete flip is exactly because location of the larger mass makes the response to the torque slower, so the tip of the top spirals in a small band instead of a near complete flip flop spiral.

It’s a simple matter stored energy along one access gradually building up enough inertia over so many rotations to finally overcome the inertia of the opposing axis.

author: @ianknight4333
text:
Is there an analogy with a pendulum? The pendulum likes to hang straight down in gravity but if disturbed will oscillate at a magnitude equal to the initial disturbance.
A body likes to spin on its 3rd axis.  If the tennis racket is launched with the intention of being spun on its third axis won't there always be just the slightest bit of error at launch causing an imperceptible wobble back and forth just like a pendulum nudged slightly off vertical.  The intermediate axis phenomenon is then just launching about the third axis with maximum error.  Rotation about the third axis is only "stable" if the launch is perfect.

There is a fatal flaw in the twisting argument. But this rotation is NOT unstable. Feynman: 1   Veritasium: 0.

author: @lqr824
text:
9:00 you seem to walk right past the most important point here: an ideal object such as you're describing would NOT have the the centrifugal force: by definition, it is ideal, and perfect, and perfectly on the graph's axis, and thus feels no force.  HOWEVER, in real life, no physical spinning object will have those light masses absolutely perfectly on the plane.  THAT LACK OF PERFECT ALIGNMENT is what causes it to have a small force... and that small force moves it to where it has a bigger force... and so on and so on and so on until it is visibly moving and quite rapidly at that.

In the video of the spinning wing nut, the more perfect the plane of spin (and the wing nut's manufacturing tolerances) the longer it would be between flips.  The more off-kilter, the faster the imbalance builds.

author: @lqr824
text: 13:30 What you're really saying here is that the earth at one point may have been in the unstable state, but has already done its change to the most stable state, and it won't change from this point because of that.  What you don't mention, and what I bet has some influence on this, is that the earth's rotation "flings out" the equator just a bit, which I believe is something like 50km or so farther away from the earth's center than the poles are (being 53 I learned this 45 years ago or so, not sure of the exact amount).  So, this probably ensures that any planet that is rotating is probably already just enough disk-like that this rotation may suffice to make that plane of rotation the stable plane of rotation.  Or in other words, if a heavenly body is spinning, that may suffice to make the plane of spin stable.  (Your examples in the space station show bottles.  But if you used a water balloon without even the filling nipple, I'd guess that if you started it spinning it'd expand around its equator, self-reinforcing that plane of spin.  So while the rigid bottle changes planes, a flexible balloon--modelling the flexible earth crust with liquid magma underneath--wouldn't.)

author: @harrisman5262
text: In around 1960 the Quaker Oats Company gave away a plastic spinning toy in packets of their breakfast cereal Oat Crunchies.  It was a hollow sphere around one to one and a half inches in diameter, with one smallish part sliced off (imagine an apple placed on a chopping board, then with a straight motion you just slice away a chunk).  From the centre of the flat (circular) part protruded a short straight rod which was perpendicular to the flat surface.  This toy was actually a spinning top, and if you spun it fast by holding the "stalk" between finger and thumb and then set it down on a smooth surface with the stalk upright, after a while it would start to topple and then suddenly flip upside down, so it was now still spinning but standing on the "stalk".  When I was at University in the late 1960s reading Maths, we studied Intermediate Moments of Inertia and I finally understood what was going on.  I wish I could get one of those toys now - it fascinated me then and I know it would do so again!

author: @claytrocious7000
text: If a solid body were rotating about its axis of lowest moment of inertia and dissipating small amount a kinetic energy,  why doesn't the body just spin more slowly about that long axis. Seems like a non-symmetric dissipation would be required to destabilizes the rotation. In the case of the satellite, the antenna flinging around turns the body into one that isn't rigid, so the theorems of rigid body mechanics no longer apply in a strict sense. If symmetric dissipation were possible, could one prevent the behavior of the body seeking its largest moment of inertia vector to spin about? It seems to me it isn't the presence of dissipation in itself, but a dissipation that isn't uniform or symmetric as a reason for the  instability.

text: I have noticed this since i was a kid, 35 years ago.  Also note the tennis racket has an air resistance dam placed across its threads.  This dam enhances the twisting effect. The wing nut in space has a thread release point which gives the nut a minor wobble.  after a twist or two the wobble will cause the nut to invert on itself...and so on...the wobble will not be lost...especially in space.

author: @seamushague958
text:
The instability of rotations about the intermediate axis is easily understood if one views the trajectories on the energy surface; see here:
https://thatsmaths.files.wordpress.com/2019/12/benderorszag-rigidbody.jpg?w=419&h=385

The figure is taken from Bender and Orszag, Advanced Mathematical Methods for Scientists and Engineers (a fabulous text, BTW).
If the angular velocity vector starts along the y-axis (the intermediate axis), since the fixed point there is a *saddle*, and therefore unstable, the angular velocity vector will move away exponentially at a rate determined by the largest positive eigenvalue of the saddle, given any arbitrarily small perturbation. Contrarily, rotations started about the 2 other axes are stable; small perturbations result in small oscillations about the fixed points. The lesson here is that qualitative analyses of ODES, through geometrical (phase space) techniques, can be a very powerful way to understand the dynamics of low-dimensional systems, without the need for complex analytic expressions, which do not yield much insight.

text: The Earth-flipping animation at 10:08 has two problems.  The source of illumination should not flip as shown but should remain constant, and the direction of rotation should be the opposite as shown post-flip.  Angular momentum in conserved throughout the flip, and in this case it should be "up" (via the Right Hand Rule) both before and after the flip.  (That's up with respect to the video -- or up toward the Pole Star, if you prefer.)

author: @MrGsteele
text: The conclusion that the earth's spin is stable is worth questioning.  The earth is not either a uniform body, nor is it a static body in terms of mass distribution.  It is, in fact, more in kind with a bottle filled with fluid - but a fluid (molten rock - magma, and both molten and solid iron core) quite viscous.  If the question is: "will the earth flip" as in North Pole-South Pole axis invert relative to the plane of circulation around the sun, the answer is a qualified no - but will other elements of the earth - the light continental crusts, drifting over the mantle and redistributing mass, the convective cells within the earth's outer core and mantle, and the instability introduced by the Milankovich forces - cause internal changes such as magnetic pole reversal.  Earth really can't be considered a solid, uniform body by a long stretch.  We have abundant and undeniable evidence of both continental drift and of frequent magnetic pole reversals over recent (thousands of years) history, embedded in the rock forming either side of the ridges on the sea floor - illustrating both the instability of the earth's mass distribution and the consequences on the magnetic polarity of the dynamo that is the earth's heart.  It would be interesting to know if the phenomenon described in this video is in any way connected with the pole reversals we've seen - and on life on earth, such as bird migrations that are dependent on navigation by magnetic polar alignment.  Given the acceleration of magnetic polar axis drift in recent years, we may be on the verge of a reversal, which would pose interesting questions on all sorts of issues, from ICBM navigation to GPS to migrations to at-sea navigation, etc. - and what I found interesting was the speed of the reversal of the spinning objects relative to the amount of time they remained in their original orientation.  A rapid flip gives less time to prepare our systems for the phenomenon and respond.

author: @hortenseweinblatt1508
text: The Earth should never have been viewed as liable to flipping. First, the Earth's situation is not that of "big mass on one axis and small mass on another axis." Even the hex-nut in the ball of clay is far too severe a perturbation from the Earth's scenario. Second, the Earth is not a solid sphere: it has fairly-symmetrical internal components that are gummy (the mantle) and liquid (the core.) These components provide drag-effects and tidal-effects that absorb (i.e., covert to heat) the momentae that would, in these microscopic scenarios of highly-rigid objects (e.g., wing-nuts) result in the flip. Third, the tidal force of our Moon similarly dampens any tendency to move towards a flip. (August 31, 2020)  Copyright 2020 by the holder of this forum-handle: all rights reserved.
