author: @MaverickSu-35
text:
Very interesting video, I appreciate it! As a fresh AE (aerospace engineer) I admit that I haven't come into knowledge about this interesting physical, yet incredibly simple phenomena (until you understand the small unexpected ingredient which creates it, it first appears weird and non-logic) until I saw this video! I'm not looking to criticize the vectors representation on the small point masses, but if they were to represent the acceleration magnitudes, those should've probably been greatest only when the disc would be at 45 degrees from the reference plane and become zero at 0 and 90 degrees. Those look only like instantaneous force or velocity vectors! Indeed, the inertial (or the fictitious centrifugal) forces of the small point masses are the greatest at 90 deg and zero at 0 deg, so they make sense as force vectors, but speaking about accelerations only, at 90 deg there is zero arm to generate any rotating moment about the X axis (if we take X passing through the great masses) and at 0 degrees there's the greatest arm, but virtually zero inertial forces to apply to the small point masses. From this it would make sense to me that at exactly 45 deg, the acceleration is highest (as there's the highest product between arm and inertial forces) and goes towards and reaches zero at 90. This also makes me conclude (if indeed at 45 the acceleration is highest) that the acceleration is a tangent function of the relative angle between the disc and the reference plane. Please correct me if my theory is wrong stating that only at 45 degrees inclination, the acceleration is highest.

This has great implications for all 3 inertial axis objects, such as space objects or even my beloved airplanes=). From this I can conclude that if a fighter plane which starts developing a pitch rate but the Y (wings plane axis) axis is having a high enough offset angle (less than 5 degrees can be enough high) from the real axis about which the whole plane is purely rotating (so not the actual pitch axis), the higher the pitch rate or the higher the offset angle (basically you can think of it as a dot product between them) will eventually make the plane flip-roll 180 degrees. This now validates why I was seeing MIG-29 OVT's or Su-35s unexpected and surely not pilot intended 180 degrees rolling motions half way through a Kulbit maneuver (a fast pitch up rate 360 degrees pitch rotation).

Here is at least one possible example (it may be due to a brief asymmetric lift between the wings as much as for our subject's phenomena) of this non-commanded effect which affects the plane's reactions, similar to the tennis-racket:
https://youtu.be/y8qmkLcXua0?t=179

author: @mataspacevicius8254
text: Idk how it took so many years to figure this out. I often observed this phenomenon flipping phones, skateboards, and knives. I used to wonder why I couldn't flip things in the air without that secondary rotation happening, despite attempts at trying to correct for what intuitively seemed an error in forces I was exerting. After watching this video, my observations make perfect sense. Seeing the rotation actually matches my new/corrected intuition. Even before getting to the part in the video where they explain how dude was able to describe the phenomenon in a way that would make intuitive sense, I had a hazy idea of it in mind. The idea came from noticing the acceleration and deceleration of the flipping motion. I was thinking it must have something to do with a very slight offset in weight distribution on different ends of the axis of rotation. Unnoticeable in the beginning but due to centrifugal force, exponentially greater. Hence, if you can make an object spin perfectly upon its intermediate axis, it would never flip. The object would probably have to be completely rigid and the flip performed in a vacuum without any other forces acting on it. Something like magnetism or air resistance could make the most minute change off axis and centrifugal force would eventually and exponentially cause it to flip, and the momentum(near insignificant initially) of that spin would carry it back to and past spinning near perfectly on the 2nd axis over and over

author: @bwacuff169
text:
The Japanese discovered this a few hundred years ago, I believe. The katana (samurai sword) operates this way ( and the tachi before it, also works this way). This is most likely the reason it's curved and stiff. You don't throw it however (you're only executing the flip portion of this phenomenon): when holding it with one hand, you can take your thumb, place it on the opposite side of the handle and then give the sword a quick push in the the opposite direction you want it to go while, at the same time, flicking your thumb sideways to give the blade spin and then let go of the sword.

A split second later, you grab it again (it takes some practice to figure out the timing but once you get it down, you can do it in basically any direction, easily) - in that amazingly short period of time, the entire two to four pound, 28 inch sword flips and is now pointing in the opposite direction it was a split second earlier. It's takes almost no effort and is one of the aspects of the weapon that made it so terrifying to face: you have absolutely no idea where the strike is coming from. Two samurai could be walking toward one another, holding their weapons one way, and in the quarter step before coming within range of each other, reposition and launch the weapon from someplace it wouldn't have seemed possible.

This technique only works with curved blades. A straight sword won't do this.

However....the longer, flexible swords, like the English longsword, had an ace up their sleeves that curved swords can't emulate.

author: @redfishervictoria
text:
A spinning objects centrifugal motion induces a temporary dielectro-magnetic geometry. This spinning motion causes the electrostatic lines of force to align and enter along the axis of rotation to the magnetic geometries center. As the spin continues the induced torque on the lines increases until the tension builds up and seeks to minimize. Therefor the object rotates 180 degrees loosing torque yet due to the continued centrifugal motion the electrostatic lines of force begin to torque.
Increased tension - release via rotating - increase tension.......

author: @mikebennett146
text: A 'perfect' rotating sphere with a perfectly balanced mass in space will never flip-over. Since the Earth is not a perfect sphere, and nor does it have a perfectly balanced mass in space, it's possible it can flip-over/reverse poles. It's a 'relatively' stable spheric mass in space (in regards to overall mass distribution from the inner-core up until the outer crustal layer). But what we're really talking about here/what matters to us is the outer/crustal layer. Since it's floating on the middle layer (Magma), it's susceptible to flipping-over/pole reversal. Deep-core ice & rock drilling samples which reveal evidence Millions of years into Earth's past have shown that Earth's poles have flipped/reversed many times in it's past, with regularity. The 'liquid nature' of most of inner Earth (Magma) surrounding the core/mantle and comprising a large amount of Earth's overall mass,  (liquid being in perpetual motion), somewhat helps to counteract the increased frequency of possible flip-over, almost like an 'internal gyroscope' counteracting the 'external gyroscope.' The Earth is also wider, and hence more dense at the Equatorial zone, almost like a spinning top. The question is does this fact mathematically further enhance or decrease it's frequency of crustal flip-over/reversing poles whilst floating on a liquid layer of perpetual motion. Counter-intuitively and going against science as we know it (in regards to spinning masses in space and the 'perfect sphere/mass' theory), it may actually help to counteract the frequency of potential crustal movement.

author: @roberthigbee3260
text:
Fantastic computer graphic showing the equally fantastic explanation by mathematician Terry Tao.  This Terry Tao explanation implies that if an object can be spun absolutely perfectly about its intermediate axis, it will not perform the intermittent flipping effect.  This phenomenon touches on a far wider phenomenon known as "design sensitivity".  Any engineer designing anything has to evaluate the effect on their device of a particular magnitude of change to any of the hundreds/thousands of parameters that make up their device, an example would be an overly touchy throttle where a tiny fluctuation of the throttle produces an unexpectedly large acceleration, or in electrical terms, to much "gain" in the circuit.  Some engineers assign "criticality" levels to all aspects of their designs.

As an adjunct mechanical engineering professor, I have been demonstrating this "Dzhanibekov" phenomenon since 2012 in a machine design course I teach (I knew nothing until today about Mr. Dzhanibekov).  I use a simple rectangular wooden block from a children's wooden block set and I color coded the sides much like you color coded your tennis racket.  Perhaps like you, I was tipped off on this phenomenon by an engineering book that stated that objects spin in an unstable fashion about their intermediate axis.  Later I, like you, I stumbled on the first international space station video you showed where an astronaut carefully imparts a spin to a special T-handle causing it to unscrew itself from a panel (maybe you saw this video first).  The fact that the T handle spends the first several rotations unscrewing itself is important to this video since that keeps the T-handle aligned such that it "launches" off the panel relatively well aligned to the intermediate spin axis, meaning that it takes many revolutions for the Dzhanibekov effect to initiate and which makes the international space station video look creepy and interesting.

I was unaware of cosmonaut Dzhanibekov and his similar spinning wing  nut observations, but there again, because his wing nut had the chance to spin about its intermediate axis in a relatively aligned fashion due to its start on the threaded rod, when it was ejected off of the end of the threaded rod, it took quite a few rotations for this effect to occur and made the effect much more pronounced and interesting to cosmonaut Dzhanibekov.

So, the key point in the Terry tau explanation is that during the flip portion of this phenomenon and for the first 90° of the flip, the lesser masses of the object in the Terry Tau explanation, which are initially centered on the intermediate axis of rotation, experience increasing forces in a direction that promotes the flipping but, and as you pointed out, the critical and fun fact is that for the next 90° of the flipping event, the forces acting on the lesser masses in the Terry tau explanation experience a decelerating force which attempts to realign them to the original spinning position about the intermediate axis at which point the phenomenon starts all over again. 

So, The more you carefully initially align the spin of the object about its intermediate axis, and the smaller the smaller-masses are in the Terry Tau explanation, the longer the spinning object will spin about the intermediate axis between flipping episodes.

Ever since I started puzzling over this phenomenon, my mind did not jump to the spinning earth issue that you say so worried the Russians (they probably were also mulling over any military applications of this phenomenon in terms of rotating projectiles including rockets, like the tumbling rocket issue that befell that early USA rocket you talked about), but to tall rectangular sky scrapers.  In my mind, I was concerned that if such sky scrapers are being spun by the rotating earth about their intermediate axis, I wondered if there was an unplanned for torque being exerted upon their foundations.  The Terry Tao explanation answers this question - yes, there is a torque and the magnitude of the torque is related to the angle of the rectangular skyscraper to the earth's spin axis (would be fun to write out the formula for the Dzhanibekov torque acting for such tall structures).  If the rectangular sky scraper is aligned correctly, the torque is zero, but there are other alignments where a, perhaps, measurable torque is generated.

I doubt that building designers know about or account for this torque, but it would be interesting to correlate problematic sky scrapers, for example, ones that experience glass cracking (due to the unplanned for torque on the building) more than other sky scrapers.   Or, perhaps the torque on a misaligned sky scraper is small and "not of engineering significance". 

On a somewhat related cool physics note, to this day, uninformed people blame the Coriolis force on everything including the direction water spins as it goes down a toilet.  Calmer minds have long ago proved that the spin direction of the vortex of water draining down through small household round drains has more to due with the directions of the machining marks left on the metal drain than whether or not you are in the Northern or Southern hemisphere.  BTW, there is a funny Simpson's cartoon episode where the USA Australian embassy, according to the cartoon show, installed hydro jets to force the toilets in the USA Australian embassy to spin the good old Yankee way (ha ha).

Thanks again for a thoughtfully produced and well explained video.

author: @helmutz.baumert4447
text:
The Janibekov effect is one of the reasons why turbulence exhibits the erratic, white-noise-like behavior we all know from our daily practice. This "white-noise"-like behavior is the basis to describe turbulence by means of so-called "Fokker-Planck equations", which are often also called "Kolmogorov equations". They dynamically balance probabilities generated by white-noise-like forces, i.e. where the correlation functions are delta functions. These three bright minds came across with their ideas almost at the same time: Fokker and Planck coming more 
from physics, Kolmogorov more from math (maybe Fokker and Planck were the initiators and K. jumped on a running train which he cleaned from "ugly observational trash" so far that clean, charming theorems came up. For details please google UNIVERSAL EQUATIONS AND CONSTANTS OF TURBULENT MOTIONS; see also  UNIVERSAL CONSTANTS  AND EQUATIONS OF TURBULENT MOTIONS. 
One even might say that the Janibekov effect (together with a number of brigtht ideas from other sides) helped to solve one of the last unresolved enigmas of classical physics: For more than a century we humans have had ALL the strict and fundamenal laws of fluid mechanics and of classical physics in general in our well-manicured hands, but could not put these pieces together to PREDICT simply observable phenomenae like boundary layers (airplanes, rockets, submarines ...) to get a simple mathematical exercise which it really is. Obviously it was necessary for us humans to go to space to discover what sits in "boring classical physics" and what also BIG DATA and AI did not discover. The human brain, sitting in our real-world body, trained by our daily life practice in our real-world environmen is still the most powerful instrument the eternal evolutionary process has ever generated. We shuuld accept that this process is sort of a wonder calling our respect.

author: @srbishb
text:
The earth has a precession wobble. It has a lot of liquid water and fluid magma interior. Additionally, there is a magnetic interaction between the earth and the sun. Given all this, the earth could indeed have a cyclical inner and/or outer flip. It certainly has seen magnetic pole shifts as is evidenced in the geologic record.
There is parallel historical records of such a physical flip. The Andean legend of the sun not rising for an entire 24 hours and the same time period biblical account of the sun not setting for an entire day. If these ancient accounts are true, it could be and likely has been already calculated.
Stretching this further, massive flooding - on a global scale would likely occur as well.

author: @wajideus4591
text:
There's quite a few problems with the argument about why the earth can't flip. First, the example for the argument is cylindrical, not spherical. The two shapes have completely different moments of inertia. Secondly, the examples don't take tidal forces from the moon into consideration, gravitational forces of nearby planets, nor continental drift. Thirdly, the earth has internal energy (a molten core) and external energy (solar radiation) that it has to deal with.
As far as I can tell, we're in no way safe from this effect. Hell, it might even explain why one of the planets in our solar system rotates backwards.

You could use the same argument for spinning around the stable axes which gives the incorrect result of flipping with those axes also which does not occur. That is a result of the solutions of Euler's equations and the only difference between the stable axes and the unstable one is one type has a minus sign and the other has a plus sign. There is no intuitive way to see that beyond the mathematics.  Your explanation(Tao's explanation) is an oversimplification.

author: @TheEulerID
text:
With the Earth and other large, spinning orbital bodies, there is a feedback loop on the moment of inertia. What makes the Earth's moment of inertia maximal at the equator is that is because there is a small, but significant, equatorial bulge. What causes that bulge? The rotation of the Earth of course. So what came first? Did the Earth spin on its current axis because that's where the moment of inertia is maximised, or was the Earth's moment of inertia maximised about the current axis because that's the way it was spinning?
I rather suspect the latter, although it's known that the Moon has a stabilising effect too.
In any event, I would have thought that feedback loop would act to stabilise the Earth's spin.

text: Your conclusion that the earth won't flip may be valid for the here and now with current surface conditions.  However, an excursion into geologic history may be necessary for a firm conclusion.  Consider atmospheric conditions that deposit many thick kilometers of ice on the north and south poles, effectively for a short time, changing the axis exhibiting the maximum moment of inertia to that  of the poles....  This condition may have been achieved in past ice ages, distributing excess mass to the poles, retaining it for a time before surface and gravitational forces return the overall mass distribution to that of the oblate spheroid we are currently familiar with.  With sufficient buildup, a planetary flip is possible.

author: @nickfraser9589
text: Strange?  Isn't this the same set of forces that keeps a rotating bicycle wheel vertical when it rotates?  Its well-known that when you replace tires on your car you have the wheel balanced before taking to the road so that the plane of rotation of the wheel is maintained to a mechanically sound level.  Unbalanced, the wheel will cease to rotate around the desired axis and cause wear on the bearings.  Of course, there's little need to rotate a wheel in space but when you do rotate an object in space alone I would think that one would get a uniform unbalance, balance, unbalance, balance effect because of the near impossibility of having an absolutely perfect mass distribution in any manufactured object.  Correct me if I am wrong, but in a zero g environment on an unfettered, unattached rotating small body, I would think the behaviour is perfectly predictable (though you will forgive me for conducting this "gedenken" experiment and speaking out of turn if that is the case).  I would be at a loss to explain it mathematically, that is true, but I've worked on enough gyroscopic instruments in my days at sea and also in the air that it simply seems we've not broadly extended our prediction of phenomenon to rotating bodies in which there are few external forces acting on it.  Mankind's prediction of the 3 laws of motion was hampered by his observation of motion in a "non-Newtonian" frame of reference and I think this is just some of the same.  Objects on earth would behave the same but they are beset by larger forces which overcome tehh object before this effect becomes visible.

author: @michaelrunnels7660
text: When I saw this video I immediately thought of the polar magnetic flip that happens every so often.  Compasses now point to the magnetic north pole. After the flip, these same compasses will point to the magnetic south pole. The magnetic poles are caused by the liquid magma rotating inside the earth's crust.  I always thought the magma somehow flipped, or reversed direction, and the crust remained rotating in the same direction.  Perhaps the magma remains rotating in the same direction and the crust flips. The video says that the earth's crust is spinning about it's maximum moment of inertia and is therefore stable.  He offers no evidence that this is true, so, to me, that's pure conjecture.

author: @Paul-rs4gd
text: I felt pretty reassured by the bit about the Earth now spinning about its axis of maximum inertia.  Then I had some doubts:  The Earth is an almost perfect sphere.  That implies that the 3 axes of rotation are not very clearly defined.  Presumably a few mountain ranges, undersea trenches etc cause the asymmetry that defines the axes.  Which leads to the question - Could a 'small' movement of mass like the melting polar ice caps cause the axes to change order ?  Thinking it through, it seems that we are still safe because losing mass at the poles should increase the angular inertia of the current spin axis.

author: @Iceflkn
text:
There is no, nor ever was a Mayan, "End of the world" prediction.  

The Mayans recorded time, basically, in complete chapters they called, "Great Cycles".  Each one was meant to be a representation of rebirth, not death.  One of their calendar systems (and they had a few) was called the "Long Count".  

These were recorded as "Great Cycles" that were 5,000 years long.  In fact, in the beginning half of 2012, archaeologist and National Geographic Grantee, William Saturno, found a series of numbers painted on the walls at a Maya complex in Guatemala showing a series of calculations for predictions reaching another 7,000 years into the future.

Why did these writings finally stop?  Basically, its because the Mayan culture fell apart so no-one was around to write any more.

author: @Thelearninghobbias
text:
Huh. This is hard to make truly intuitive. My attempt:

why doesn’t the object just slow down gradually, rotating on the same axis the whole time?

Well, that spinning would dissipate the most kinetic energy the fastest. 

Why is that even a problem though??

Well, objects in motion want to stay that way. A car rolling forward wants to just roll forwards. If you want to dissipate the kinetic energy of the car faster, you have to apply a force to the brake pedal to force the system to maximize dissipation of kinetic energy. Otherwise, dissipation of energy is naturally minimized - a ball traveling in a zero gravity, radiation free, vacuum would not slow down. Kinetic energy dissipation, or, conversion (to heat energy/any other energy) is naturally minimized if left alone, to the extent that it will never convert any kinetic energy at all unless forced to by electromagnetic or other radiation, or something like air resistance (or brake pads being forced together). 

So the object rotating with the weight concentrated at the pole is spinning fastest, like an ice skater spinning with their arms and legs tucked into their center of mass.

This fast spinning maximizes energy conversion from kinetic to heat and other energies. But as we know, energy conversion like that is minimized naturally, it won’t even occur at all unless it’s forced to. So with the fast spinning causing the most energy conversion, it naturally becomes unstable like a car with the brakes being pressed. That car is unstable in that its motion will change rapidly (to a stop) until you let the brakes go. To the spinning object, the brakes are heat dissipation, slowing its rotation most appreciably. Therefore, just like if you let off the brake pedal, the brakes will naturally come away from the wheels even if they weren’t pneumatic. It is the same - the brakes are heat dissipation slowing the spinning, so the “brakes” naturally float off, still sliding but now minimizing contact now that there is no applied force to the pedal. 

In this case that means that the sloshing of the water is naturally reduced, just like the friction of brake pads to the wheels would be naturally reduced when you stop applying force to the pads (through the brake pedal). Because nothing is “holding the brake pedal down”, or “forcing the state of highest dissipation/instability”.


This probably applies to solid cylinders experiencing rotational air resistance on an unstable axis, but occurs much faster, and/or in a vaccum, to cylinders filled with liquid. 

That’s my guess. Just a laymen

author: @Groundhog4634
text: That might be intuitive under the assumption that there will be something that bumps it every time it flipped.  Here is my hypothesis. The bigger point mass in your example will move side to side in the same plain as the biggest moment of inertia. Because it is unstable. This side to side movement causes the small point masses to move in a spiral. As the acceleration grows bigger in the spiral the force on the small point mas grows bigger. When it gets big enough the object will flip over. Initially, the "flip" is assisted by the side movement of the bigger point masses as it moves through 90 deg. The side to side force opposes the "flip" force. Dissipates the "flip" force until it is zero. We are now 180 deg from where we started and our spiral starts again. Like you. I am fascinated by the intermediate axis theorem. I think proving that an axis is unstable is not describing the movement of the object or axis. That is why physicists find it impossible to describe in a simple manner. I think there is a relationship between the difference in the inertia of the intermediate axis and smallest inertia axis, in terms of how fast the spiral grows. In other words how fast it flips. The unstable spinning top a good example of the movement of the unstable intermediate axis. I hope this is a simple explanation of the movement of the unstable axis. Hope this helps :)

author: @Five_y_kay
text:
The centrifugal force occurs due to momentum. The weight of whatever traveling in a circle has velocity, and wants to travel in a straight line, but its forced to go around in a circle. It is constantly getting pulled towards the center (by structural strength of what ever is spinning) changing the direction of the chunk’s velocity vector to be tangent to its path. If it where to rip off and fly away, the chunk would spin on its center of g at same rpm as whatever thing it was ridding and travel in straight line which happens to be tangent to the circle it was going around in, right where it broke off. Very related to this is gyroscopic stuff, and also, orbital mechanics.

Not to mention, you can aim a sling if you know this stuff.

author: @Mithgaraf
text: Sorry, you mix two different phenomena. Rolling over in space can be explained very simply. This is a very ordinary phenomenon. Motion relative to one axis is added to motion relative to the other axis. It seems sinusoidal to us, but it is not. It's circular. The moment of apparent stabilization results from the fact that the center of mass is located at the heavier end, which also performs movement in space. Accurate video analysis from the space station clearly shows this.

author: @terenfro1975
text: It's not micro gravity.  It's free fall.  If acceleration due to gravity is 9.81 m/s^2 at sea level and acceleration due to gravity from the earth is 8.73 m/s^2 at the space station, then it is not micro gravity.  The only thing that is missing on the space station is the normal force from objects pressing back due to gravity, hence it is free fall.  This is why your apparent weight is 0 N.  No normal force.

author: @cantcode1001
text:
Anyone not sure why the wing nut still flips after being stabilized by the bolt, I have an explanation: At the very end of the bolt, where the thread terminates, is a single point where the thread is flush with the end of the bolt. 180 degrees away from that, it is not. So as the wing nut reaches the end, there is a moment right before the wing nut frees itself completely that the thread is applying force to only part of the wing nut. This uneven force just as it makes that last turn off the bolt, induces a slight wobble.
You can see this in the video.

author: @amzarnacht6710
text:
Wow... such a simple thing overcomplicated.


Take your spinning bicycle wheel:  It really wants to maintain a fixed orientation.
Now, take something (the wings on those nuts, the center post from that T-handle) and it adds a third axis of rotation that is in tandem with the primary rotation but extended outward from the center of rotation.  As the wheel spins, that third balance begins to oscillate and, when it reaches a point where it's rotational torque equals or exceeds the rotational torque of the primary axis - it flips.  It looses rotational energy when it flips and allows the primary axis to regain it's rotational stability... for a short time, as the third axis of rotation balances and begins to oscillate again...

Three separate laws for something so simple?

text: Wait though hold up. Olympus Mons, Ascraeus Mons, Pavonis Mons, Arsai Mons, and on the opposite side, Terra Sabaea, form the bulk of the highest points on mars (thanks world globe in Surviving Mars), with vast areas of deep valleys and plains on the surrounding land in many areas elsewhere on the planet. Mars is a smaller body, so having all that mass concentrated at it's equator makes sense. My question is, what happens in millions of years, when the continents on earth move in such a way that the current concentrations of mass along the axis of the equator shifts significantly? What happens when the Hymalayas no longer exist where they are today (or get larger/smaller), and Africa, North and South America, and the rest of the continents, start to re-coalesce elsewhere? Has earth been undergoing these kinds of orbital shifts over geological timescales while moving along it's orbital axis with the sun? The moon should also be theoretically totally unaffected since the center of gravity itself doesn't really shift. Does the fact that the earth is still mostly liquid in the mantle, vs the geologically dead mars, also have an impact on this effect, as shown in the video? So many things that didn't get covered that I have questions about now!

author: @degustibus
text: Fascinating video, thank you for all the work on it.  Please don't mind a couple quibbles/questions.  Perhaps you could do a presentation of the equinoxes and gyroscopes (any footage of gryoscopes in space?).  In the horrible quake that caused the monster tsunami that hit Japan and Fukushima nuclear plant the force involved actually redistributed enough mass of the earth away from the center to ever so slightly, but detectably, speed the Earth's rotation and shorten the day.   Now that was the 4th most power quake recorded with modern instruments, so one does have to wonder what are the plausible upper limits of geological events that could change the planet's motion.   Quakes and volcanism are two obvious considerations, but then there is the possibility of a significant mass hitting our moon (increasing its mass or greatly decreasing it) or us directly.  Thanks again.

author: @Thomas-es5nn
text: This is misleading though. You make it sound like the twisting must occur whenever a object with 3 axes of inertia is rotating along its axis of intermediate inertia. You say, "... there is virtually nothing you can do to stop it." But at the time mark of 8:22 of the video, you clearly explain that the twisting happens BECAUSE its rotation is NOT perfectly aligned to the axis. This is what you say, " ... what if the disc is bumped, so that it is no longer rotating perfectly about the Y axis." That is when the twisting will occur. In other words, the twisting doesn't necessarily occur whenever it is rotating along its axis of intermediate axis, like you seems to be saying,  but it is only conditioned on the fact that the rotation is NOT perfectly about that axis. And we can clearly see that when you throw the tennis racket or the phone. You are NOT throwing it perfectly "straight" and the racket or the phone is ALREADY slightly twisted when it leaves your hand. If this is the case, the phenomenon isn't really as bizzare at all like you are saying.

author: @destrygriffith3972
text:
Ok, might sound egotistical, but i am six minutes in, and i basically think i've got it. I mean, it just seems intuitive to me. Nothing is perfect. No spin is without perterbation. But here there's a balancing effect. A flywheel. But still, in essence you have a balanced rod on a point: easy in principal in a wind-free environment. But impossible in practice. Always an imperfection. Slightly more one way or another.


Well then think chaos theory. Chaotic systems (which is really any system outside of a lab: think the three-body problem) always build up perterbations. Black swan theory. Things are balanced until they're not. The flywheel corrects the minor perterbations until they build up to a big enough one, which they do on a regular interval, different for each system, dependent on the geometry and velocity (initial conditions, but with limits and constraints).


This might all be a load of crap, i dont know. Not a physicist. Let alone a classical mechanics specialist. Just a knee-jerk reaction. A gut intuition. It gets out of balance but doesnt just start tumbling. It pulls the flywheel into a new, temporary stability.


Like bouncing. We could look at it as just a complex bouncing ball.


Of course the ball suddenly changes directions. When it hits the ground it doesn't go everywhere, it goes into a new, stable net momentum. And then gravity smoothly brings it back to the ground via a continuous deceleration, really only looks like it changes direction on paper. And then the ground flips it round again.


Like the rotational momentum gets transferred to the axis material when the perterbation gets big enough and IT becomes the flywheel. But then of course the main mass takes over, the bigger mass, and that becomes the flywheel again.


It seems to me that something like that is happening. It would be interesting to hear from someone who understands this better if i'm on the right track or not.


Not interested if someone doesn't really understand what i'm saying and just wants to correct my verbage.

author: @ApolloWasReal
text:
One of the best explanations I've seen, but you could have said even more. Because of its spin, the earth has an equatorial bulge; points at sea level on the equator are 21.3 km farther from the center of the earth than sea level points at the poles. (Mt Everest is only 8.85 km high). This greatly increases the stability of the earth's rotation. The poles *do* move, mostly in a circular motion with peak amplitudes of a few hundred meters due to redistributions of mass in the mantle, oceans and atmosphere (including melting glaciers) but otherwise the spin is quite stable.

The planet Jupiter, which is almost entirely fluid, has an even more pronounced equatorial bulge because of its very fast spin.

text: An easy and fun way to experience this effect is tossing a cereal box.  Three orthogonal axes, the mass of the box symetrically distributed around each axis of three.  Toss it in the air trying to spin it only about one of the axes.  Practice just a little and you'll be good at spinning it only around one axis.  However,  you will find that you can successfully spin it in stable rotation around two of the box's axes, but there will be one of the three axes around which, try as hard as you will, and practice all you want, the box simply will not spin in stable rotation.  Kitchen science!

author: @LovingTinha
text: You would still want to keep an eye on things, just because something is spinning around its axis of maximum moment of inertia currently, doesn't mean it isn't gradually shifting over a long period of time, such an object would take much longer to shift and flip. And the earth's solid shape under the water is not perfect to sustain this consistent spin. We know it has flipped before due to this. And will likely flip once again in time. But over time the frequency has undoubtedly lowered, as such an event would cause shifts and changes towards balance. We are likely closer to a balance than ever. But if there is even gradual shifts happening. Then it is inevitable. Sorry to bust that safety bubble. But we have to be realistic and not lie to people. The earth would require perfect symmetry to avoid such a shift. And it simply doesn't have that. And even a perfect symmetrical object can be shifted if foreign matter or force is introduced to tip the balance.

author: @vernonpeterson3323
text: I may have not understood it as well as I do now but as a construction worker years ago I observed and demonstrated this phenomenon. I would take my framing hammer, (or any type of handled tool) hold it by the handle with the weighted end away from me with claw and hammer face to the left and right direction respectively I would then quickly flip the handle with a quick upward motion so that the handle could be caught again when it had made one full revolution. The claw and face would always exchange positions. That is, if the hammer face was to the left and the claw was to the right before I flipped it they would turn I/2 revolution so the face was to the right and the claw was to the left when I caught the handle after flipping it one full revolution.

author: @don67a
text:
Interesting. I'd actually noticed this one some tie ago, fiddling with a T-shaped object, and spinning it as shown while tossing it in the air, and noticing exactly the same motion - apparently spinning "upright up", then "upright down", with rapid transition between the two states. I have now idea how many times I tossed that thing trying to work out what was going on! In the end,  I came to basically the conclusion you describe - that the middle of the T accelerates rapidly through the plane of spin and only slowly toward the poles, giving the illusion that it's flipping between two stable states.


(I should point out that my university mathematics career ended in stage 1 with the words, "what math test?", so I was thinking about the motion without actually doing the math, just an intuitive idea of how the accelerations work - pretty much the same line of reasoning as you describe...)



Had no idea that it baffled rocket scientists!


How dos this relate to inertial coupling, the effect that caused (or contributed to) the loss of the X-2 in 1956? My understanding is that if an aircraft is rolls "off axis (i.e. the axis of roll is not down the "long axis" of the aircraft, the ends will tend to accelerate outwards - that's OK on most aircraft with long wings, where the acceleration of the wings counters the off-angle acceleration of the nose and tail. With an aircraft like the X-2 where the wings are stubby and thin (and light), the acceleration of the nose and tail overwhelms that of the wings, causing the aircraft behave like the T in our little experiment, and if not damped by the empennage and control surfaces, the aircraft can violently swap ends, just like our spinning T-handle. 


I thought that effect was pretty well studied by aerodynamicists in the mid-late 1950s - in fact Mel Apt was working on that exact problem until he was killed in the X-2, and the same affect nearly killed Chuck Yeager in the X-1A in 1953.


Or have I missed a trick on this one? (Apart from the fact that airflow makes it all more complicated...)

author: @physikstudent3628
text:
Couldn't one also argue with the conservation laws?: Because the energy E and the angular momentum L has to be concerved and E=L²/2J, the absolute value of the moment of inertia J of the current axis has to be constant.
In case the moment of inertia is at  a non-degenerated extremum (1st and 3rd axis of a rigid body), there is only this one momentum that has the current energy. So there is no possibility to change the axis without changing the angular momentum or the energy. These axis must therefore be stable.
But in case of the 2nd axis there are different axis that have the same moment of inertia, so the rotation axis can change and will change if there are any deviations.

This also explains why the water-filled bottle has only one stable axis: Now, the rotational energy is not constant anymore but can reduce due to friction. Therefore, the argument from above is not valid for the 3rd axis anymore and only the 1st axis will be stable.

author: @Eltaurus
text:
This is a very cool video and it provides by far the best summary of the history behind the effect.
However, the proposed intuitive explanation is, unfortunately, wrong.

It is easy to see because the same reasoning would assert that rotation about the minimal axis is also unstable, which is not true.
The reason for this mistake is that you cannot make conclusions about the stability in non-inertial reference frames without taking into account the Coriolis force - it actually can change the stability of a problem. Stability of Lagrangian points L4 and L5 in a three-body problem may serve as an evident example of that.

author: @travisgould7653
text: So, can we apply chaos theory here? In Tao's simulation with the different point masses, the disc will only flip if there is a small bump. That means the tiniest of instabilities in the beginning of the spin will grow into big instabilities that cause the unusual flip. Therefore, it may be possible to spin something on the 2nd axis, as long as it remains completely stable. But that complete stability on the 2nd axis may be near impossible to achieve. Since even a single atom more or out of place on one side of the axis would be an inherent cause for instability. So, for a perfect 2nd axis spin the object must have an equal mass distribution around the 2nd axis and each atom must be identically placed to its corresponding atom on the other side of the axis. But Tao's simulation already shows this.

author: @chopmega
text:
Angular momentum is I omega.  That is, it is the moment of inertia times the angular velocity for the axis about which the object is spinning.  The angular momentum is constant because there are no external torques acting on the body.  So if you compare angular momentum about the different axes, you get:


H = I_1 omega_1 = I_2 omega_2 = I_3 omega_3


Now the angular kinetic energy is 1/2 I omega^2.  That's very analogous to linear kinetic energy, 1/2 m V^2.  


Notice that you can substitute the constant angular momentum into this equation for angular kinetic energy.  You get:


KE = 1/2  H omega


H is the constant angular momentum.  So this lets you see why the kinetic energy is greatest when spinning about the axis with the minimum moment of inertia, because that has the highest angular velocity.   And when you spin about the axis with the maximum moment of inertia, you have the lowest spin rate.  So that gives you the lowest kinetic energy.  That's why the body will always end up spinning about that axis if there is any way for the body to dissipate energy.  


The flexible antennas on Explorer 1 did that.  Any wiggling of the original spin axis caused those antennas to flex.  as they flexed, they reacted moments back into the body and those moments change the angular velocity of the body.  It's almost like a precession caused by those tiny moments acting on the body if you think of that body as being separate from the antennas.  And similarly, the reaction moments acting on the base of the antennas caused them to wiggle.  So the bodies and the antennas interact and drive each other whenever there is wobbling of the spin axis.  


The flexing makes heat and the heat radiates out to space.  So the original kinetic energy gets converted to heat and the body ends up spinning more slowly about its axis with greatest moment of inertia.

author: @greenaum
text:
Just a little nitpick... you mention "microgravity". While this is a common term, the gravity experienced by astronauts is almost full Earth gravity. Things just float about because they're in freefall, constantly falling. It's like being in a falling lift, in a long enough lift shaft things would seem like they were floating. Or in the "vomit comet" aeroplanes like in this video, where the aeroplane dives and it seems like gravity is switched off. 

In orbit, a spacecraft is constantly falling, pulled toward the Earth by gravity. It's also constantly moving forward, with the inertia from the movement it's engines gave it during launch. If you just launch a rocket straight up, it falls straight back down again. So rockets tilt over and start moving sideways, at 90 degrees to the Earth, then start moving that way. 

So they fall at the same speed everything else does. It's just that when they've fallen, say, a metre, they've also moved forwards a bit. Enough that the Earth's surface, being round, is a metre further beneath them. This keeps going, through inertia, Newton's first law, keeping the craft moving forward, and gravity keeping that inertia from sending the craft off into deep space. The gravitational pull and the forward movement are in balance. 

(I realise Mr Veritasium knows this)

Point is a lot of people, as I used to, think there's just less gravity in space, cos it's too far away from Earth. That's not the case. "Freefall" is a much better description of what's going on, and just the phrase explains a lot. I think it would be good if science educators used the phrase more, "zero gravity" and "microgravity" are misnomers. It's less _effective_ gravity, but the same thing happens to anything that's falling. Since that is what's happening.

author: @GordOlson
text:
Interesting! I noticed this behavior many years ago as a kid, trying to figure out and understand certain skateboard flip tricks. I have no formal physics education, I just like to think about things… but it seems to me that there is a hierarchy of stability in rotational axes, where mass = stability. In the disc example, the 4 points of mass rotating around Z is always the most stable, with the least amount of inherent wobble. And comparing the X and Y axes, where there is mass on the axis line, the one with more mass on the axis line will always be the most stable. So in this example, Y, the intermediate axis, is the weakest of the 3 rotational axes, because there is less mass on the Y axis line than there is spinning around it (on the X axis). This allows centrifugal forces to cause the wobbling mass on the Y axis line to run away from the axis, and thusly to rotate around the next strongest axis in the hierarchy, which is X.
Ultimately though, it should come down to uneven weight distribution and wobble. In theory, under perfect conditions, an object could spin around the intermediate axis without flipping... An absolutely perfectly symmetrical object, with perfectly uniform density, in a zero gravity vacuum, spun perfectly on that axis without the slightest amount of wobble, would rotate without flipping. But we can't create those conditions, so I think there will ALWAYS be a wobble in ANY object’s rotation, which is why this effect always occurs. 
Also, why do you say that centripetal force is the only force acting on the mass points? Isn't that incorrect? I don't believe that centripetal force actually draws mass inwards, centripetal force is simply the amount of force required to resist the outward pull of centrifugal force, is it not? Centripetal force is not the same as gravity. 
I don't know, am I out to lunch on all of this? I’m kind of interested to know if the way I’m thinking of this makes sense to those with some actual education on the subject! lol

author: @RomanVladimirovichF
text: The explanation of Terry Tao, although it seems clear, but in fact it is not. Indeed, the reasoning given to explain the instability of rotation around an axis with an “average” moment of inertia can be applied in exactly the same way to rotation around an axis with an “least” moment. But, however, the rotation around the axis with the smallest moment is stable. This means that the explanation is untenable.

author: @johnmacleod2487
text:
Seems like a few intelligent people here in comments. I'll throw out something I've been thinking. Earth is oblate, fatter at equator. If left alone, not rotating, gravity, acting in all directions, would leave a sphere. With no gravity and rotating, would theoretically flatten out into a disc. As it gets fatter at the equator it loses angular momentum, and slows the rotation. Think figure skater putting arms over their head, pulling as much mass into the center to speed rotation - but the opposite


So...what I've been thinking...what if it gets too fat, rotation slows down so much that it stops f(l)attening. Balance of forces then switches to gravity, pulling the oblate mass back to spherical. Then speeds up again, because less fat, then gets fatter again, slows down, repeat. 



This sound plausible to anybody here? Might explain one or more of looong geological cycles? As the magma core cools and solidifies, cycles would take longer, be less cataclysmic

author: @frankveluzable
text:
Its not as complicated as your explaination.
We know that when an object with 2 masses rotates in any axis then the centrifugal force of each mass is always parallel to each other. There are 2 condition of alignment:
1) The centrifugal forces are perfectly aligned to each other.
2) The centrifugal forces are not aligned to each other. 

In condition 2 the tendency of 2 centrifugal force is to aligned to each other due to the bending created by each centrifugal force to each arm from the center point of rotation. When each masses reached a condition where each centrifugal force are aligned it will not stick to that perfect alignment because there is a momentum of each masses that make is pass the perfect condition aligned condition. This momentum put the 2 masses in a pendulum effect so it will go back and forth to alignment then non-alignment condition repeatedly. 

I suggest you to study the pendulum effect in a rotating masses. That is the reason why it is hard for you to understand the characteristic of a rotating body. Pendulum effect if the only thing you neglected to consider that makes the rorating body hard to understand. I can explain with 3rd and 4rth masses in addition to what I explained.

author: @jamesolivas9234
text:
Uh... I'm a little confused why the host states that he is going to try to explain this, then passes up the obvious example for a simple explanation.

And the visual for this simple explanation is right there in his video.
Watch the spinning "T" handle with a steel shaft at 5:14 in this video. 
( https://youtu.be/1VPfZ_XzisU?t=314 ). 
The object is spinning about the axis which passes through the shaft, but not perfectly. It has a slight “wobble”.
The “wobble” will start to magnify due to the nature of centrifugal force, as can be seen by the spiral shape the end of the shaft draws in space. This spiral continues to enlarge until the force of the “wobble” overpowers the inertia of the “T” shape, at which point THE SHAFT becomes the greater point of inertia. 
However, the rotational inertia is not alone. It also comes with linear inertia. 
I don’t understand the math, or which terms to use, but simply said, the linear inertia is pushing the shaft toward plane in which the "T" is spinning, and when the “spiral” falls directly in line with that plane, the linear inertia keeps pushing the shaft “forward”, which is the FLIP this video is attempting to explain.
And it continues to happen because the imaginary “spiral” continues, but now it is decreasing in size.
Eventually the decreasing spiral approaches zero, but the same imperfection which caused the centrifugal force to start the spiral to begin with, will cause the spiral to miss a perfect alignment with the shaft, so the spiral starts to increase again, and the cycle repeats.

text: i find it more likely that in small bodies filled with liquid, it's probably not so much that the liquid diffuses energy through heat, but that the liquid near the top and bottom of the container- where the small irregularities in rotation are most pronounced- is pulled by inertia to the outside of the container. this inertia moves the top and bottom of the container further to the outside, resulting in a 'flatter' spin. this process repeats until the container is spinning around its third axis, with the liquid mostly at one end or the other.

text:
So the phenomena occurs because the earth is rotating or the object is orbiting the earth and that creates the inertia.  The rotation (of the earth or orbit) creates the "bump" you talk of at 8:33 in your video.  So, this phenomena is also related to the Coriolis effect which occurs for basically the same reason. 

Also, as a result, both the Dzhanibekov Effect and the Coriolis effect may not occur in deep space travel...which might make it impossible to grow plants due to the the lack of the Coriolis effect helping plant chlorophyll to photosynthesize.

author: @gorisenke
text:
Maybe their end of the world fear was made by a lack of thought? In not a great physics mind, but all of the liquid core tests shown were done with objects that had a premade rigid shell that liquid was injected into. 

But the earth doesnt meet that standard. It was a rotating liquid mass that cooled and developed a hardened shell around the preexisting motion while moving in that same direction. So the body squashed and pulled mass towards the greater moment of inertia, so it should have already stabilized. And even though it's a weaker force, I would think the earth's magnetic fields would keep the ferrous cores moving roughly the same way.

author: @dhy5342
text: The tennis racket and cell phone flip over  because when they're flipped, the hand doing the flipping unconsciously induces a spin.  It's nearly impossible to throw something without spinning it.  Things spun mechanically will flip because of their inherent imbalance.  One side will almost always be slightly heavier than the other and, when spinning, the heavier side will in time induce it to flip.  If a perfect, uniform density sphere is spun, it will not flip.  If any other uniform shape of uniform density is perfectly spun on any of its three axes, it will not flip.  In short, if the thing being flipped begins its spin exactly through its precise center of mass, it won't flip.  Or course, when something is flipped in air, the air will act on it to throw it out of balance.

author: @giggadygoogog
text:
I remember asking why the earth was wider in the middle when I was in school. I was told that it got wider across the equator due to centrifugal force. That never sat well with me. 
Moment of inertia theory suggests that the earth spins the way it does because it is wider across the middle, but it is wider across the middle because of the centrifugal forces created by its rotation.
The theory of maximum moment of inertia explains why it is wider in the middle, but now I am thinking that wider in the middle v direction of spin seems to be a chicken and egg thing.
Proof of we were indeed created by ancient aliens in coalition with martian "Egyptians".

author: @xaytana
text:
What's even more interesting about this, is the motion of the 'flip.' How does it conserve so much momentum during this?
From the outside, it has the same direction of rotation throughout it's linear path. But from the perspective of the object, rotation comes to almost a full stop before it tumbles, then it proceeds to rotate in the opposite direction; essentially from the perspective of the object, the motion is forwards and clockwise, then a tumble, then backwards and anticlockwise. 
There seems like there is also a lot of momentum conserved here if an object can do multiple flips.


This is also a great example of just how imperfect our universe is. What works on paper doesn't always work in practice. No better way to show this than a wobble induced by the smallest of forces that aren't detectable with our own eyes.








Now for other things.


Looking at the perspective of the object, could we actually use this as a design implementation for space travel? Rather than having multiple smaller 'reverse' thrusters, you could induce a roll, flip the craft around, stop the roll, and use the main thruster as a brake. I know spacecraft are much more complicated than any object demonstrated in this video, but the working principle could make it possible to do this with a more complex object, right? 
Something like this would be interesting for a constant takeoff and reentry vehicle. Say it's conical with a dished bottom. It'd be aerodynamically efficient for atmospheric takeoff, and doing the flip would make reentry possible, and could use the thruster for landing. This would reduce the amount of auxiliary thrusters needed for maneuvers like braking and flipping the nose and tail to opposite sides. I know this isn't the best example, but lets say the craft approaches reentry with it's nose before flipping, to make this a valid use case of the concept; which would also work in the case of traveling to multiple bodies, whether they have an atmosphere or not.
I know this is a lot of what-if's, speculation, ideas, and science fiction since we don't have a space craft that can take off without massive amounts of thrust and fuel. But assuming in conditions where something like this could be possible, could we use this effect/theorem to simplify spacecraft? 


I also wonder if a a craft, with the appropriate auxiliary thrusters, could stop mid-tumble, to point the nose of the craft in a different directional axis.

author: @mikemalo6336
text:
you can do a trick similar to the tennis racket flip: take a pair of solid metal scissors that have been loosened to where they are almost sloppy on their axle. hold the scissors by the blades, closed. 
1.  toss them upwards to about head height, giving them a slight flip so that they flip over once and you catch them again in the palm of your hand. easy, get the hang of that so you consistently can do one flip and they land  flat in your palm. 
2.  now when you do the flip, give the scissors a spin along the long axis so you make them do one flip and one twist before landing flat in your palm.
3.  Now do it again but harder so you get 2 or 3 flips & twists before landing. And then you'll see the cool part, the scissors will open and close 2 or 3 times while in the air and land closed, flat in the palm of your hand. You will get the 3rd axis spin without trying.
you should now also have a good feel for the balance of the scissors, try throwing the scissors like a dagger, you should be able to stick them in a drywall sheet from any distance!! (Be careful, Don't run while holding scissors and, ask your mom before throwing scissors, sticking them in your bedroom walls.)

author: @siggyretburns7523
text:
I dunno if I agree with that. Although its comforting, there's a wobble effect that takes place even in a balanced spin. When a top is spun, it spins until friction gets the better of its angular momentum. At that point, the top wobbles a few times then straightens back briefly, then wobbles even more again until friction and gravity wins.
So while Earth may not flip, angular momentum will eventually lose its battle against friction and cause Earth to wobble.

author: @SihAza
text: The tennis racket spin about its axis could be either cw or ccw, due to symmetry.  If it always rotated one way, that would violate symmetry.  There must be a slight torque applied about the axis to cause it to spin one way or the other.  Symmetry is the highest rule in Physics, and why the Twin Paradox has never made sense to me (in an empty universe with no absolute coordinates).  Ditto for a bathtub vortex, it can spin either way depending on the slightest initial condition.  With a perfect torque applied, the tennis racket should rotate without any spin.

author: @michaelmoore7975
text:
The objects flip because not only do you have a linear, axial, and radial rotations, but also because it is moving very fast through space (as we all are) on a huge curve with a much smaller curvilinear orbit; this influences the smaller masses to instability and recovery, instability and recovery, and so on. 
                                                       *OR* another way to say:
Relative to everything around it, the object moves in only the 3 axes, but the reality is there is a fourth and fifth movement unaccounted for, because the object along with everything around it is moving very fast through space along a huge curve with a curvilinear orbit.
   I guess we could also add the movement of the Solar System through space to it as well.

author: @onebronx
text: I remember one of my friends in was a bit frightened in 1990-2000s by a theory that the global warming will cause all Greenland ice to melt, which might cause a great redistribution of the Earth mass, change in moment of inertia and the flip. But it seems like it works opposite way: the liquid water from molten ice caps will tend to gather near equator due to centrifugal forces, thus raising the major moment of inertia and stabilizing the rotation even more. If the mass of water on the surface could be enough to cause the flip (which I doubt), it would be rather due to buildup of huge polar ice caps during ice ages.

Couldn't it be that the Earth is flipping and has flipped many times according with The Effect on the course of its history, but since we're always looking at Earth from the perspective of its maximum moment of inertia, we have a hard time seeing the flips across the space-time? And the supposed "pause" in movement and redirection of the Earth's rotation on the apex of the flip could be explained by our perception of time through Quantum Physics. I know, our planet turns from the left to the right and there's no sign it ever went the other way, but that too could be something explained solely from our perspective on the surface of the lithosphere. Actual signs of flipping are only detectable for us today through our measurements of the Earth's magnetic field and/or gravitational anomalies, not on a conventional spatial way. I don't know, I'm obviously not a theoretical physicist. But I suppose there could be something there along these lines. We're just not looking at the big picture yet, or we're not looking hard enough, or thinking outside of the globe-shaped box. It might be just a question of perception, but we're flipping alright. You say it is impossible, but skateboarders have done it. 
No it isn't the "The Impossible."
It is called: "The Monster Flip."
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wCucgxqIRiA


Also if you want to flip your phone that way, it is more of an arm trick. 

You raise your arm up, with the phone laying in your four fingers under the phone and your thumb above the phone

author: @ZagrosŞêxbizin
text:
My physics professor’s ( Mario Freamat PhD. )response to my email when i had sent him a link of the astronaut’s video 9 months ago. Hi, Zagros.
Nice find.
The clip illustrates a classical result of the rotational dynamics of an object with three unequal axes of symmetry (like a rectangular block of uneven sides), called the "intermediary axis theorem". It is more like a corollary of the model describing the rotation of an object as a set of coupled rotations projected about three rectangular axes of symmetry. It states that, while the rotations about axes parallel to the largest and smallest sides are stable under small perturbations, the rotations about the intermediary axis will be unstable. That is, if the object is spun around axes parallel with the larger and smallest sides (like the length and thin height of your mobile) and the rotation is slightly perturbed, the perturbation will remain a stable precession. Yet, if the rotation is about an intermediary axis (like the width of the mobile), a perturbation will be amplified and the object will flip the axis while rotating. The cause is that the moments of inertia (that is, how the mass is distributed about the axis of rotation) about the three axes are unequal, with the one about the intermediary axis in the middle. The math is not immediate, but it can be shown that the character of the perturbation is given by the differences between these moments of inertia. For each axis, the difference involved is between the moments about the other two, such that the difference will be positive when the rotation is about the intermediary axis, and negative otherwise, which changes the physics of the equations describing the perturbations: exponential growth about the intermediary axis vs. harmonic precessions about the other two. You can try to observe the phenomenon for yourself by tossing your mobile rotating in the air above a safe surface: note how the rotation is smooth when about the long and thin sides, but wobbly when about the intermediary side.      
The object in the video clip also has three axes of symmetry, and note that the astronaut spins it about the intermediary axis. The object would spin smoothly if the astronaut managed to put it in motion exactly around the respective axis of symmetry, but he didn't, so the rotation is perturbed from start. The perturbation is amplified until the axis of rotation is flipped. After the flip, since the rotation is still perturbed due to inertia, the axis is again flipped. Because the experiment is in conditions of levitation, the motion looks like an oscillation of the spin.  Otherwise, if done in normal conditions, the flip would be harder to observed because the object would fall to the ground.

author: @lukaszsternak4869
text:
The intuitive centrifugal-force explanation works well for understanding intermediate axis instability, but it breaks down when applied to the stable axes. Let me explain.
Consider the example of a thin, rigid, massless disk with four point masses (two heavy and two light). If you rotate the system about the x-axis — the axis with the smallest mass moment of inertia — and then "bump" the disk slightly so it no longer rotates perfectly about that axis, the large point masses will experience a small centrifugal force. According to the same logic used to explain instability about the intermediate axis, these forces should cause the point masses to accelerate and trigger a flip. However, since rotation about the smallest moment of inertia axis is known to be stable, this leads to a contradiction.

So why does the centrifugal-force argument fail here?
Does the intuitive explanation only apply to the intermediate axis?
Can someone clarify this?

author: @Platyfurmany
text:
NOW I UNDERSTAND WHY, in 2010: Odyssey Two, the American Discovery spacecraft was windmilling when the Russians in the Cosmonaut Alexi Leonov arrive at Jupiter!!!  This one thing has bothered me ever since Arthur C. Clarke published his sequel to 2001: A Space Odessey.  The Dzhanibekov Effect explains so much about what was going on, and how when the rotation of the spinning carousel (to simulate gravity for the astronauts on the Discovery) slowed to a halt, that spinning motion around the X-axis of the Discovery ultimately converted to a windmill rotation around the Y-axis of the ship.  Thank you, Derek, for clearing up that mystery for me!


Oh, by the way, would this also be a good explanation for the semi-regular flipping of the Earth's magnetic poles???

text:
I don't understand your explanation of why the earth's water makes it immune to this? You compare it to asteroids, planets and other rocky rotating bodies, but they have no liquid so I don't understand the connection?  The vast amounts of liquid water here certainly makes since and could account for the rarity of life on other planets which should flip too often to allow for life. But exactly how this works is incoherent in your description. 
Those bottles are filled with liquid while the earth only has a thin surface layer. I assume the liquid inside the bottle tends to remain still and resist the rotation until friction forces at the outer edge balance out. That's why the bottles quickly slow the rate of rotation, when spun up quickly. Put the bottle on a motor and let it spin until the liquid inside is spinning at same rate, then release it and I expect a VERY different effect. However, all these aspects are completely incomparable to earth, where the surface liquids are all spinning in harmony with earth and there is no boundary friction at all? 
I learned from somewhere as a child that the function of ocean waves and wind is to stabilize the rotation of the earth. I took that to also be aided by the moon, which is the source of all complex wave dynamics as it creates the largest wave (the tide) in precise rhythms every day. Is there info that can expand on these thoughts?

author: @michalchik
text:
good video, I have a compatible but different way of intuitively thinking about it. 

Here is how I came to think of it after spending a while thinking about it when I saw my first intermediate axis book flip demonstration in my classical mechanics class. 

Both momentum and kinetic energy need to be conserved. They can't appear or disappear.

The major axis rotation has highest angular momentum to kinetic energy ratio, while the minor axis rotation has the lower angular momentum to rotational kinetic energy ratio. There is no other axes of rotation that can conserve these two quantities, so they must stay stable near those axes.

Around the intermediate axes, you are not at a minimum or maximum for either of those quantities. There are an infinite number of solutions, a small percentage of which are tumbling solutions that require the object to turn around. Since most of the solutions that preserve these quantities are near the intermediate axis, it looks like it spend most of its time stably rotating about the intermediate axis, but its really shifting through those possibilities in the process of turning through the slight end over end rotation that leads to the tumble and is imparted by imperfection in the original orientation.

author: @NigelRCharman
text: Angular momentum is not conserved. Imagine a gun firing a bullet in space,  at a railway sleeper. It fires the bullet, angular momentum is zero. It hits the sleeper,  not in the middle, but towards one end. Suddenly we have rotation, and angular momentum. What was a zero sum system now has a gun travelling backwards, but not rotating, and a sleeper, with a bullet in it, rotating. No conservation, there.

author: @TruthNerds
text:
8:15 Centripetal forces *do not* balance centrifugal force. I know that everybody (*not literally) says that, but it is wrong, in general and typically, and shows some deep confusion about how they relate to different reference frames and the motion of objects inside those frames. 😅

Centrifugal force is a "fictitious" (I prefer "inertial" or "non-Newtonian") force that applies to every object off of the axis of rotation in a rotating reference frame.

Centripetal force is a placeholder for any force that keeps an object in a circular motion around a point in _any_ reference frame, rotating or otherwise. However, most commonly it is properly applied to an _inertial_ reference frame because an object experiences no circular motion _in the corotating reference frame._

Typically, you are either looking at:
- circular motion in an inertial reference frame, in which case a centripetal force is needed _but centrifugal force is zero_ (as you say yourself)
- no motion in a corotating reference frame, in which case centripetal force does not apply but centrifugal force is non-zero.

TL;DR: Centripetal and centrifugal typically apply to different reference frames under consideration and therefore balance in neither frame.

author: @UnderstandingSystems
text: Great video, loved the animation. However, this logic implies that the same effect would occur when the mass rotates about its axis with the smallest moment of inertia. Some important details are missing - namely, the Coriolis force. When you are in a rotating frame you must account for both the Centrifugal force (proportional to mass and the distance from the axis of rotation) as well as the Coriolis force (which increases with mass and velocity, and acts in a direction perpendicular to both the velocity and axis of rotation). Basically, when you rotate about the intermediate axis, the Coriolis forces are negligible because they are proportional to the small masses, and pushing the small masses in a direction perpendicular to their motion and the axis of rotation requires the large masses to move as well, while the Centrifugal force only requires the small masses to move. Therefore the Centrifugal force dominates and you get the effect explained above. Now, when you rotate about the axis with the smallest moment of inertia, a small perturbation of the large masses (which are now along the axis of rotation) results in a Centrifugal force as well as a  Coriolis force, just like before. However, this time the both the Coriolis force and the Centrifugal force are proportional to the large mass, and it is no problem that the Coriolis force requires the small masses to move as well, because they are negligible compared to the big mass. The effect is that the Coriolis force is large enough to move the masses in a direction perpendicular to their motion and the axis of rotation. A force that stays perpendicular to the direction of motion results in circular motion. So basically, the Coriolis force is able to turn a small perturbation of the large masses into a small circular motion about the axis of rotation, and it doesn't let the Centrifugal force dominate, which would result in the flip.

author: @digitalscribbler68
text:
This is a nice explanation, but perhaps it doesn't connect with our intuition. Not mine, anyway. The most striking thing about the flip is the apparent correlation of the rotation and flip frequencies. We might better call the effect "disproportional precession." Maybe somebody already calls it that. But another striking thing about it is that the minor disruptive forces acting through the smaller bodies in your animation apparently wouldn't cease to have their effect no matter how fast or slow the body is spinning.

Regarding a potential flip of the Earth, the moon must necessarily be included for its stabilizing effect in the system. The moon does some physical magic for us that maybe we haven't fully appreciated in the past.

author: @TacJackal
text:
I'm by no means a physics expert. Even the explanation was difficult to follow, but I thought I'd mention the perspective I had before learning of this video's explanation. Maybe it that might add context to visualize it better.

5:15 When the "T" shape rotates, the wings spin counter-clockwise. Even though the entire T flips, the wings still spin CCW to our perspective. That means the entire T is flipping on the axis that runs between the wings (which doesn't change the axis where the wings are spinning from) . But what is making the stem of the T flip like that? It only flips after a certain amount of turns, meaning it has to be a progressive force that keeps building in magnitude until it shifts the balance of the stem around. It's a weak amount of force or torque who's effect is smaller than the primary spinning axis, because it flips less than the T spins. Because it does not inhibit the spinning, and it always shifts back and forth consistently without destabilizing, it has to be a balanced and constant force that doesn't loose energy.

In other words. This flipping is stable and continuous so it must be a natural result of the object spinning, think as though it's shifting its position like it's taking the least possible 'path' of resistance. If the stem of the T is slightly destabilized, then it's like knocking a top or a gyroscope off center, where the spin starts to pull it apart, only this time there isn't a table to stop it from flipping and pulling itself back together.

author: @randomsandwichian
text:
Though it may not count, I think this has similar properties to spinning a Tippe top; you may use the stem to initiate the momentum of its rotation, but it will always flip to spin on that said stem until it slows down.

The similarities are;
a. 1 direction of spin and inertia (in the wingnut, away and parallel to the bolt, for the top, gravity)

b. the distribution of its central axis of balance (irregular wing nut and the top-heavy Tippe top design)


My hypothesis; the smaller the ratio of differences of the object's central axis of balance (from a wingnut to a bead), the higher the frequency of its flips (ie. the least stable its rotation along its axis) in zero G.

author: @robs7110
text:
The tennis racquet phenomena and the rotating wing nut, although related, are not the same. The tennis racquet has one-half flip for every rotation. The wing nut continues to rotate many times with increasing instability, then flips for momentary equilibrium followed by increasing instability, ad infinitum. This explanation appears to address the wing nut but NOT the tennis racquet. 
Also, there is no explanation for the direction of flipping in either case. Why does the flip only seem to occur clockwise when viewed along the flipped axis?

I think this phenomenon is just the extension of Gyroscopic effect. If you look carefully at the end of the video where the rotation of the T-shaped object is slower, the object begin to flip when the rotation is unstable.

author: @ultimatewestyexperience4345
text: I appreciate your videos a lot. I thought I might just add that in reality, and contrary to public opinion, 'centrifugal' forces don't pull objects directly away from the centre of rotation as you say at 8:15. If you cut a hammer thrower's line, the hammer would not suddenly take a 90 degree turn directly away from the thrower - there is no force applied to do it. Instead it just carries on in the same direction as it was already going until a different external force is applied. Yes, the hammer thrower thinks that the hammer is imposing a force away from him, but that's only because the line is taught. Therefore the idea of centrifugal force is only 50% true when you take components of force into account. Maybe you could do a video on this!

author: @Fuddleton
text:
There's an easier explanation. Think that things are imperfect - you'll never have pure rotation along one axis only. 
Spin on the "heavy" axis, and imperfection would give a little spring to the "light" axis. But the light actually is too light to have an effect. 
Spin on the light axis, and it's so light that it's easy to make it spin very fast, and fast things don't change easily (angular momentum).
Spin on the intermediate and it's easy to give spin to the light axis that it becomes unstable.
Also, this works if you flip your phone.
