author: @totherarf
text:
I can see complications with planets and this effect!
You can think of a planet as being a point source of gravity with a revolving hollow sphere of crust. Because of the rotational energy being transmitted to the crust it will tend to have a greater radius at the equator than the poles! This means that the spin imparts energy to counter gravity at the equator ...... but this is a function of the spin and gravity.  Alter the spin or the gravity and it is unstable. 

On a solid planet there would be no problem , but the earth’s crust is floating on a somewhat liquid core of magma .... which is floating on a more liquid core of iron! 

We know the magnetic effect of the spinning liquid iron (or maybe the solid centre) is moving! This means almost certainly that the inner core is altering its axis of spin! We also know that the Pole star (true north) has historically not been in the same position! This means that over time both the inner and outer parts of the globe have been spinning at different axies! Unless there is a self protecting effect happening it means that we may well have some interesting times ahead!
Weather us humans are still around to witness this possibility  is moot!

text: Comments - if the paper was peer reviewed,  how is it possible they dropped such a clanger as saying Landau and Liftshitz did not mention it when they did ?  Lazy reviewer ? .  2.  Your explanation does not address the issue of why the tee handle  stays stable for a short time and then after x rotations becomes unstable and flips - what provides the upset , and why indeed is there a short time of stability ?  So  there is a fair bit more to it.  8:44 "Tension forces within the disk ensure...." Bollocks.  The formal analysis treats it as a rigid body. Internal tension has nothing to do with the dynamics...just fluff.  Why did the Russkies keep it a secret ? - I'd say that it  is more do do with the reentry stability of MIRVs and possibly some short range missile stabilisation systems that they wanted to analyse (plus look for defects in US or UK systems) before they made the matter public domain.   I'd be guessing  Feynmann knew of the issue from the tennis racket throw effect because  you really need to have a space station environment in order to see the  wingnut/tee bar effect .

author: @SternLX
text: Maximum moment of inertia. Fun fact: When I was in the USAF we had to measure the Maximum Moment of Inertia(via a hanging Sway Test) of every bomb type that was planned to be tested on the B2 Bomber during it's initial flight testing phases. They needed to know how much time it would take for the flight control surfaces to stabilize the flight of the falling bomb when it was released. Each bomb only has 2 lugs holding it to the bomb rack(one forward and one aft) and they don't always release exactly at the same time. Only takes Milliseconds to induce an inertia pitch or yaw.

author: @creativewebmedia-uk
text: I love your videos however I do wish to voice a point about Centrifugal Force....There is no such thing! Hold on ...please don't shout me down "Yet" What we call and know as (Centrifugal force) is actually a lack of Centripetal force. when you use the garden game where a ball is retained on a pole by a cord which allows you to hit the ball which then rotates in a circle caused by the centripetal force on the string holding it back from traveling in a straight line. Which is the direction preferred by any force eg a straight line. Therefore what we call centrifugal is actually the result of force trying to travel in a straight line which inevitably makes it travel outwards from the center of a spinning object. for so-called centrifugal force to be observed contact with two surfaces have to be made. eg; the object fixed and spinning and the other object unfixed and in contact with the other. I hope this has been helpful. I am open to opinions on this .

author: @tejing2001
text: After mulling this over for a bit, I realized there's an issue with this explanation: as stated, it seems to apply just as well to the principal axis with the smallest moment of inertia. For a complete intuition, you would need to understand why it DOESN'T happen there. I believe the answer to that question will probably center around the coriolis force on the 'flipping' mass, and how the torque from that affects the primary rotation. That torque should be small in comparison to the primary rotation's angular momentum in the intermediate axis case, but large in the smallest axis case.

author: @dwilliams2068
text:
I remember this from high school,  circa 1950.  I would bind a book into a "solid"  with rubber bands and spin it about the max and minimum moment axes and then challenge a friend to spin it about the intermediate axis and of course it tumbled out of control.  "Well, I spun it about two axes, max and minimum.  Why can't you spin it about the in-between axis?"  I didn't have the slightest idea of what was going on but sort of thought that if a "perfect body" were set to spin with impossible perfection in the middle axes it might not tumble.  True or not?

Later in college we had a class in dynamics that analyzed the behaviour of a spinning bullet.  The result was amazing.  I followed the lecture at the time but could never repeat the analysis.  

Ain't physics fun?

I think the demonstration of the liquid in the bottle spinning on the short axis, then flipping to an 'end over end' configuration of rotation, is the clearest example of the support of a 90 degree pole flip on axis.  I noticed when I was a kid and playing with gyros that they tended to do the same thing. 
Further, when momentum is lost upon a particular axis of rotation and a flip occurs, if the object is reenergized to the same rotational velocity, it would then maintain that orientation until momentum is once again lost and it flips to an opposite or adjacent axis. With the magnetic impact of the galactic wave sheet encountering our star system as it does every 12,000 years, I believe that would be sufficient energy impulse to provide rotational velocity to maintain stability on that axis until it's next flip occurs with momentum degradation on that subsequent rotational axis orientation. 

Since the only solid portion of earth is it's crust and the rest is plasticized material in a semi liquid state in the case of the mantel and core zones, then add in the oceans which area about 70% of the planet surface, it is logical to expect a 90 degree flip on axis as was demonstrated by the liquid filled bottle in zero g environment. 

Therefore, I believe your conclusions that the earth will not flip on its axis is incorrect.  If you believe my logic path incorrect, prove me wrong.

author: @astdrwings2573
text: So, it flips over because of its minute instability on one of its axis. The thing is, nothing tells us that there is absolute stability on the other two axes, and they never flip over on those axes. Against my own thinking, if the object seeks stability by rotating about an axis where it has maximum moment of inertia, nothing says that the object sought stability about one axis where the masses around it are equally distributed, so the moment of each segment of mass keeps changing while constantly seeking their own axis. The dominant one pulls the largest which results in oval shape locus. Just like the earth going round the sun, its oval. Its the principle of conservation of energy that can be used to satisfy Kepler's law. The earth is for sure not balanced, or at least no one can be sure of that. As for the wing nut, it calls for a lab experiment to create a perfectly balanced wing nut, spun around a ball bearing and see its behaviour. And for the liquid bottle, we should bear in mind that liquids change their centre of gravity with acceleration, all because it constantly is in the state of convection and different densities co-exist.

author: @derbigpr500
text: The tennis racket theorem is so idiotic it blows my mind that some scientists actually take it seriously. All that's happening is the forces you apply to the racket (or anything else) when trying to rotate it in air lengthwise aren't perfectly balanced and make the racket rotate like that. Any small offset of where the force you apply is center on the object you're throwing will make an object not spin in air in a balanced way, it's obvious. Build a machine that will rotate the racket PERFECTLY balanced, and it will not make a half turn in air.  In fact, you can do it by hand as well if you practice enough. How do I know?  Played tennis for almost 20 years. I've probably spun a racket like that a thousand times. And just to prove the point, I just did it with my phone like 6 times in a row after getting a hang of it and finding the right way to throw it.

author: @Wizarth
text:
I wondered if the flipping behaviour would still be observed in a vacuum, without air currents etc to introduce the instability. But I then realized that no object is going to be spun perfectly around its axis, and any uneven mass distribution or gravitational anomaly (due to uneven mass in the surrounding environment) would be enough to introduce the stability again.


That said, it's remarkable that the flipping appeared periodic. I assume it's due to the nature of the objects mass distribution, and different objects would have different periods. But I'm just speculating!

author: @memetb5796
text: Incidentally: gymnasts convert forward/backward flip inertia into twisting inertia using a very similar method. When they are "flipping", they are spinning along the axis with the most moment, and to twist all they need to do is "change shape" enough to transfer some of that kinetic energy to the 1st axis. I'm not exactly sure it qualifies under the same theorem or problem though, as gymnasts are inherently non-rigid shapes and it becomes more of a controls theory problem. However, the bleeding of momentum from one axis to another is definitely the case.

author: @christianpeterson2678
text:
This is a great video! However, I would argue for an easier, and much older, explanation to the Dzhanibekov effect based on the Binet ellipsoid. It Turns out that in the body frame, the rotating frame of reference that exhibits the centrifugal forces, angular momentum can be used to form the surface of a sphere and the kinetic energy can be used to form the surface of an ellipsoid (A 3D Ellipse). Since in the torque free motion of a rigid body requires that both angular moment and kinetic energy must be conserved, then path of the motion, technically the angular momentum vector in the body frame, must lie on the intersection of the ellipsoid and the sphere (See GIF). It can be seen that the intersections form closed ring motions (i.e. stable rotations) around the axis with the smallest and largest moments of inertia. However, around the intermediate axis the intersections do not form closed circles, they cross all the way around the ellipse indicating that the motion is unstable and flips back and forth periodically. So the reason that the instability arises is because these two "competing" quantities (Angular momentum and Kinetic energy) must both be conserved at the same time. 

Here is a GIF and Video to show visualizations.

GIF: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/79/Intersecting_ellipsoids.gif
Video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ei79Y_aqrm0

edit: I love your videos by the way.

author: @Malidictus
text:
Bruce Yeany (look him up, he's on YouTube) has a lot of physics toys for his students. Among them are a lot of complex pendulum arrays. As soon as I saw the footage in this video (which I'd never seen before), it reminded me of their behaviour. When you have a pendulum mounted on a pendulum, movement will "transfer" from one to the other. One pendulum will move quickly while the other remains fairly still for a while, then they flip rapidly and the opposite happens.


My instant assumption was that the same is happening here. The bolt (or racket) is spinning in both axes because nothing is perfect, but in the more stable one predominantly. After a while, balance flips to the other axis, causing the bolt to flip, before switching back. The underlying physics is absolutely fascinating, but you CAN demostrate a similar phenomenon by tying a weight on a string and tying another weight on a string off of that.

author: @MendTheWorld
text:
If the Earth _could_ flip, or if it had _ever_ flipped, this would be evident in the geologic record.  No geological evidence, _ergo_ no flip.
    The geological question of interest, though, is what is the effect is of the transfer of mass from the ocean surface to the poles during glacial maxima.  it's not a small amount, but could involve the transfer of as much as 400 + feet of water, which, over the entire surface of the oceans is a hell of a lot of mass.  According to the explanation here, this would destabilize the rotation intend to make the Earth flip 90° to maximize its moment of inertia.  I haven't done the calculation, but it's obvious that the oblate shape of the [quasi-] solid Earth involves a far greater amount of mass than the mass of water shifting from the ocean surface toward the poles.  I can't remember the quantities exactly, but I believe the oblaticity of the Earth is on the order of 10 miles, which, depending on which layers are involved (or likely _all_ of them!), would involve much more mass than the ice caps (also considering the difference in density between rock and ice)
    I think the change in moment of inertia would be sufficient, however, to produce a measurable change in the length of a day, with the Earth speeding up in its rotation during glacial Maxima, and slowing down during glacial Minima.  my guess is that it would be pretty small, but I'm sure this calculation has already been done.  Too busy right now to look it up, and the time limit for my procrastination break is now used up.   Anyone else?

author: @TrueThanny
text:
Maybe it's just me, but it seemed dead obvious, intuitively, what was going on.  Rotating on that intermediate axis makes the object's position on the minor axis unstable, so that a tiny nudge in any direction on that minor axis will be accelerated until it rotates completely around that axis at least once (the system needs to lose energy so that the initial nudge isn't carried over to jump start another rotation).


If you could toss the racket absolutely perfectly (in a vacuum so you don't have to worry about eddies in the air), it would rotate only on the intermediate axis.  Give it the slightest nudge after that, though, and it would do at least one rotation on the minor axis.

author: @WillN2Go1
text:
Good stuff. That Mars spins in roughly the same direction as the Earth-despite it's uneven distribution of mass strongly suggests we don't have to worry about the Earth flipping around. And with the exceptions of Venus and Uranus, and tidaly locked bodies (or resonant ones like Mercury) most other planets spin similarly to the Earth, and Mars, easily modeled by a right-hand-rule (mine) where if thumbs up is North, then the forefinger points in the direction of orbit and the fingers wrap in the direction of rotation; this suggests that the Mars correction was not like your freely rotating body (with the cubes) but affected enough by orbital mechanics to instead move the dense spot to the position of least energy, and not flip the entire planet. 

 Other related aspects to this. Gyroscopic precession (Which Walter Lewin of MIT says "Is the most counter-intuitive concept in all of physics...including quantum mechanics".)  And did you forget to say that in a perfect rotation with no off-axis wobble then the flip won't happen? 

Also a spinning top will often go through stages of increasing and decreasing wobble as it slows down.  So a spinning top not touching a surface would instead of wobbling and correcting, flip? 

What I also like is how your goal is to find an intuitive explanation. This is what Feynman was really good at. Einstein I would argue did the same thing as well. If you can understand something intuitively than two things are possible. First, you might be able to use it in normal life and second, it makes the more complicated scientific and mathematics understanding more accessible to those working at the highest levels. Einstein didn't start with complicated mathematics and worked backwards, he first visualized. Imagine anyone prior to Newton even knowing where to begin thinking about gravity in more than a practical sense.  With gyroscopic precession I think what I observed in a judo demonstration in a Japanese high school was a practical application of gyroscopic precession (without understanding the concept).  A student spun his arms which turned his body to his left allowing him to plant his right foot and keep his balance. Had I not pursued finding an intuitive understanding of gyroscopic precession I'd have never spotted it.

author: @Андрей_ча
text:
Все подобные эксперименты проводятся в газовой среде, которая неравномерна. Это оказывает разное влияние на рычаги вращения. Поэтому любой небольшой перекос сопротивления среды приводит к вращению. Если параллелограмм, у которого центр массы совпадает с центром предмета, не может стабильно вращаться вокруг средней оси вращения, то тем более любой другой предмет не сможет этого сделать. Оказывает влияние разница длины рычагов на оси: если ось вращения проходит через длинный или короткий рычаг, то вращаемый предмет ведёт себя более стабильно. И ещё один аспект: человек не может задать идеальное вращение, даже если предмет будет идеально сбалансирован.
А на Землю оказывает влияние Луна, которая удерживает ось вращения Земли.

author: @HyungnamGu
text:
Why it Happens (Simplified):
In space, with no external forces, the total angular momentum is conserved. 

When spinning around the middle axis, small perturbations (wobbles) cause the object to move slightly off the axis. 

This tiny shift, combined with centrifugal forces in the rotating frame, causes the object to deviate further, leading to the characteristic flip, explained by equations showing unstable harmonic motion around that axis.

author: @BeingFireRetardant
text:
Just a simple construction worker here...
(Not advanced at math, physics, etc. by your understanding of such things.)
But we lowly builder types do odd things with our tools, like absent mindedly flipping them in our hands, mostly as a tactile reflex...

Hammers, channel locks, wrenches, all spin along the longest axis of rotation. But I also have a tool, that I've carried daily for ten years...

It's called a 5-in-1, a painter's tool like a scraper, with a roller shaped cut out. When you flip it, it does exactly what the video describes, it rotates along two different axis simultaneously. 

I've always attributed it to being somewhat perfectly imbalanced, but never fully understood why till now. All I know is that it does it every single time I flip it, without fail.

This does make me wonder if this is the exact reason that some bullets "keyhole" a target, rotating across the long axis, instead of spiraling as designed. What is odd, is that the keyholed bullets are as accurate as the spiraling ones.

I'm sure someone smarter than me can math that out for proof. Interesting stuff.

text: Without doing any math you can observe the cause of the phenomenon in this video. Notice when the big thumbscrew on the space station exits the threaded hole. Notice when the tennis racket is being flipped a small amount of rotation along the long axis (in line with the handle) is inadvertently being introduced. I would like to see an experiment under controlled conditions that avoids introducing the secondary force. Of course the experiment would have to take place in zero gravity. I would like to see how a hex nut acts in similar conditions. Will it also flip repeatedly? Probably not because, the tennis racket, and wing nut’s centers of gravity are not centered on the midpoint of its XYZ linear midpoints.

author: @waperboy
text: The spinning wingnut effect was immediately intuitively apparent for me - the wingnut or its axis of rotation was not perfectly symmetrical. I could not have predicted the exact (initially baffling) behaviour given those facts, but once I saw it, the logic quickly clicked into place. Once you think of rotation as the attempted straight motion of each atom of the object, having their trajectories acted upon by the bonding forces of adjacent atoms, rotation isn't so strange anymore.

author: @alexholker1309
text:
I interpreted the effect as kind of like being balanced at the saddle point of a surface. It might not move on its own, but any slight disturbance will cause it to roll downhill and up towards the next saddle point, like a skateboarder in a halfpipe.
Tidal forces act like friction, causing the mass to lose energy before reaching the next saddle point and fall back down into the valley.

author: @dachanist
text: There's a more intuitive method for understanding the flip. It lies in the periodicity. Musicians will understand. If you try to play two notes at the same time on a brass instrument, your lips will vibrate at both frequencies provided that they are harmonics of one another. That is, your lips will vibrate at C, then at G, Then at C again, and so on. You will not be able to control the duration from an equal rhythmic phasing. It is really no different than using a sum of sine waves to crate a more complex wave-shape - its the manifestation of the sum of the energy at the various points in time (just over 3 dimensions instead of 2). Since the rotation is equally unstable on both sides of the wingnut, the energy has no choice but to rotate equally with whatever periodicity is inherent to the distribution of forces. Arithmetic is not required for understanding this, only for predicting it.

K=½Iω² <<Rotatinal Kinectic Energy = (1/2)*(Moment of Inertia)*(Angular Velocity)^2>>
L=Iω <<Angular Momentum = (Moment of Inertia)*(Angular Velocity)>>

So If you (for example) halve "ω" and double "I", you would have a smaller "K" while conserving "L".

author: @darrenmarchant1720
text:
this is mentioned in the book "The Signs Before Judgement Day" By Ibn Kathir. About the Author
Ismail ibn Kathir (1301 1373) was a highly influential Muslim Muhaddith (narrator), Faq h, Mufassir, and Historian. He was the author of Signs Before the Day of Judgment. He is also well known for his Tafsir, which is widely considered authoritative. He was born in Mijdal, a village on the outskirts of the city of Busra, to the east of Damascus, in the year 701 A.H. and was taught by Ibn Taymiyya, Al-Mizzi, Ibn Qayyim, Al-Dhahabi, Ibn Al Firkah, Isa bin Al-Mutim, Ahmed bin Abi-Talib, Ibn Al-Hajjar, Baha Ad-Din Al-Qasim bin Muzaffar bin Asakir, Ibn Ash-Shirazi, Ishaq bin Yahya Al-Ammuddi, Zahriyyah Shaykh, and Muhammad bin Zarrad. Upon completion of his studies he obtained his first official appointment in 1341, when he joined an inquisitorial commission formed to determine certain questions of heresy. Thereafter he received various semi-official appointments, culminating in June/July 1366 with a professorial position at the Great Mosque of Damascus. Ibn Kathir wrote a famous commentary on the Qur'an named Tafsir al-Qur'an al-'Adhim which linked certain Hadith, or sayings of Muhammad, and sayings of the sahaba to verses of the Qur'an, in explanation. Tafsir ibn Kathir is famous all over the Muslirm world, and among Muslims in the Western world is one of the most widely used explanations of the Qur'an today. he is also known for writing the history of man from Adam to the time of Mohamad (RSA)

author: @ИванСнежков-з9й
text:
Feynman is still right. This explanation is incomplete. It does not explain why there is so little wobbling of the rotating mass.
The explanation says that small offset of the small masses causes centrifugal forces that flips them. However we observe that the object is mostly rotating without visible increase of these offsets, we don't see the masses rotating into higher and higher offsets (wobbling), the whole flipping is kind of done into a single rotation.

It's obvious that there is some balancing of powers and threshold that has to be passed so the small masses centrifugal forces are able to become big enough to flip the object.
To really understand this phenomenon, we need to understand where the threshold comes from.

11:25 This is very clearly angular momentum at play. When gas or liquid rotates around an axis, such as in Saturn's rings, the particles are flattened into a disk. In the video the bottles are turned to allow the most amount of liquid to spin at the equator.

That being said, its no example to prove that the phenomenon could happen to Earth. I think if it were going to happen it would have happened by now. And if it were common, wouldn't we see the other planets or even the sun flip?

author: @MrJoelkowalski
text:
Is it possible under scenarios of super massive ice accretion at the north pole, like the Laurentide Ice sheet, to change the axis of least moment of inertia of earth? If the massive weight of the ice cap caused the earth to tip after triggering a critical threshold of mass / min. moment of inertia… could this in theory explain the sudden melting and then flooding seen in the outflow channels on North America as the ice is subjected to equatorial heat as it forces a axis of rotation change? 

Its interesting to imagine the mass of water in the oceans (which would have corresponded with a proportional depletion of mass in the oceans) transported to northern ice sheets, then earth tipping, melting catastrophically, dumping huge amounts of fresh water into the areas around North America, which would stop the AMOC in its tracks, trigger the younger dryas cooling, and earth then wobbling back to its current “stable” rotation - until the phenomenon repeats in the next ice age.

In theory, what amount of mass at the poles would be required to do this? 

Fun stuff to think about.
